title: Jump in mainland System Frequency, coincident with potline trip at Portland smelter
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Jump in mainland System Frequency, coincident with potline trip at Portland smelter" posted at 28-08-2024 08:07:00 AM is: A system frequency jump in the VIC region was triggered when the APD No1 and No2 potlines at the Portland smelter unexpectedly tripped at 0001 hrs on 28th August 2024. Although no load shedding was instructed by AEMO, the demand reflected a noticeable drop after AEMO became aware of the incident. The incident was categorized as a non-credible contingency event, and the potlines were subsequently returned to service by 0200 hrs the same day. The root cause of the trip was identified later, and AEMO decided that the event would not be reclassified as a credible contingency due to its unlikely recurrence. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term. Its root cause is: Potline trip at Portland smelter identified post-event. Its accident scale is: Limited to Portland smelter potline tripping. Its dates are: 28-08-2024 (event occurred at 0001 hrs, resolution noted by 0200 hrs). Its affected region is: VIC region. Its affected key users are: Industrial users, particularly operations at Portland smelter. Its cause type is: Operational/electrical issue at a specific facility. Its causes are: Tripping of APD No1 and APD No2 potlines at Portland smelter; subsequent frequency jump in the electricity system.

title: (Update of) long-term trends of (the high points of) Aggregate Scheduled Target
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "(Update of) long-term trends of (the high points of) Aggregate Scheduled Target" posted at 27-08-2024 05:50:00 PM is: The article discusses the latest updates from the GenInsights Quarterly Updates 2024 Q2, focusing on the Aggregate Scheduled Target (AggSchedTarget) metric, which serves as a proxy for the need for Firming Capacity alongside Variable Renewable Energy (VRE). The report highlights the trends over recent quarters and years, noting a decrease in annual minimum and average AggSchedTarget levels, indicating challenges for existing and new firming capacity, including coal and potential nuclear power options. Despite these decreases, the peak demand points suggest the continued need for firming capacity to match peak grid demand. The article also notes that the first half of 2024 has already seen more frequent high-demand dispatch intervals than several previous years. Upcoming data from AEMO's 2024 ESOO report is anticipated to offer further insight into the requirements for VRE and firming capacity. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, with a focus on trends observed in 2024. Its root_cause is: Increasing reliance on VRE and the need for firming capacity due to decreasing firming asset viability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 2024 Q1 and Q2, specifically focusing on high points in January, February, and June 2024. Its affected region is: NEM-wide (National Electricity Market, Australia). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and market operators in the NEM. Its cause type is: Market dynamics. Its causes are: Increased VRE integration, cold weather driving higher demand, poor wind output.

title: Curtailment (spillage) of Large Solar reaches 2/3 of total resource, on Saturday 24th August 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Curtailment (spillage) of Large Solar reaches 2/3 of total resource, on Saturday 24th August 2024" posted at 27-08-2024 03:16:00 PM is: On Saturday, August 24, 2024, a significant curtailment of solar energy occurred across the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia, where two-thirds of the solar power generated by large solar farms was not utilized due to reduced market opportunities. The conditions were unusual for winter, resembling the warmth of spring, resulting in high solar resource availability but limited market uptake. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate, with potential short-term impacts on energy prices. Its root cause is: Excessive solar energy generation amid lower demand or market restrictions. Its accident scale is: Widespread across NEM. Its dates are: 24-08-2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Solar energy producers within the NEM. Its cause type is: System. Its causes are: Unusually warm winter conditions increasing solar generation and limited market opportunities for solar energy utilization.

title: Coal-fired unit performance has improved markedly from the ‘dog year’ that was 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Coal-fired unit performance has improved markedly from the ‘dog year’ that was 2022" posted at 26-08-2024 04:15:00 PM is: The performance of coal-fired power units has significantly improved since 2022, where unavailability rates reached a record high of 24%. By June 2024, the rate had dropped to approximately 18%, with more improvements expected as units like Callide C resume operations. This improvement follows a challenging period marked by extended outages and varying performance among different units. The analysis considered only the remaining 44 operational coal units across Queensland, New South Wales, and Victoria, excluding those retired such as Hazelwood and Liddell. Exceptions noted include discrepancies in outage data reporting between local analyses and AEMO's methods. The findings suggest a promising trajectory towards better availability rates amid ongoing scrutiny of coal units' operational reliability; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Coal-fired policies. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Mid to late 2025. Its root_cause is: Improved operational performance and resume operations of key coal units. Its accident scale is: N/A. Its dates is: 26-08-2024 04:15:00 PM. Its affected region is: Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria. Its affected key users is: Energy producers, regulators, and analysts. Its cause type is: Operational performance improvement. Its causes is: Decreased unavailability and improved performance of coal units, Return to operation of Callide C units.

title: Eraring Unit 3 offline for planned outage … but no more outages planned for Eraring through to 2027 (yet?)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Eraring Unit 3 offline for planned outage … but no more outages planned for Eraring through to 2027 (yet?)" posted at 26-08-2024 02:22:00 PM is: Eraring Unit 3 at the Eraring power station came offline on August 25, 2024, for a planned outage expected to last until November 10, 2024. This planned event follows the return of Eraring Units 1 and 2 after forced outages earlier in the month. Notably, there are currently no other planned outages for the station until its eventual closure by August 2027, although this situation may evolve as planning continues. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term, from August 2024 to November 2024. Its root cause is: Planned maintenance. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 26-08-2024; 25-08-2024; 10-11-2024. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational. Its causes are: Planned outage for maintenance.

title: AEMO ends ‘East Coast Gas System Risk or Threat Notice’
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO ends ‘East Coast Gas System Risk or Threat Notice’" posted at 26-08-2024 10:28:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has lifted the 'East Coast Gas System Risk or Threat Notice' due to favorable weather conditions and reduced gas demand, which have allowed for replenishment of gas storage at Iona and Newcastle LNG. This development reflects changes in energy dynamics as weather conditions improved towards the end of winter. However, lingering concerns about gas production and infrastructure on Australia's east coast continue to be highlighted by energy executives. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Gas pipeline failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, as the notice has been revoked, allowing for normal market operations. Its root_cause is: Favorable weather conditions and reduced gas demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 19th of June, 2024 to 26th of August, 2024. Its affected region is: East coast of Australia. Its affected key users are: Gas consumers and energy market participants. Its cause type is: Weather and demand change. Its causes are: Favorable weather conditions and reduced gas generation demand leading to increased gas storage.

title: GenInsights Quarterly Updates for 2024 Q2 released
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "GenInsights Quarterly Updates for 2024 Q2 released" posted at 26-08-2024 09:34:00 AM is: The detailed review of 2024 Q2 by Global-Roam Pty Ltd and Greenview Strategic Consulting highlights significant improvements in coal-fired unit performance since 2022, continued analysis of long-term trends in the Aggregate Scheduled Target, and concerns about the adequacy of replacement Firming Capacity. This analysis aims to provide insights into the National Electricity Market (NEM) as 2024 progresses. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing changes anticipated throughout 2024 based on the analysis. Its root_cause is: Ongoing developments and observations within the NEM and electricity generation trends. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 22-08-2024 (review release date) to 28-08-2024 (sharing of excerpts). Its affected region is: Primarily Australia's National Electricity Market region. Its affected key users are: Stakeholders and decision-makers in the electricity market sector. Its cause type is: Analytical and observational. Its causes are: Improved performance of coal-fired units, analysis of aggregate scheduling targets, and challenges in firming capacity.

title: It could be a high wind yield week ahead in the NEM?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "It could be a high wind yield week ahead in the NEM?" posted at 22-08-2024 01:21:00 PM is: The article discusses the forecast for strong wind yields across the National Electricity Market (NEM) due to expected polar cyclones, which may result in multiple consecutive days of damaging winds and gales starting from Western Australia and moving eastward. This is seen as a significant departure from the wind lull experienced in the second quarter of 2024. The highest predicted wind yield would be slightly below the all-time maximum reached earlier in the year. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Approximately 9 days starting from 24th August 2024. Its root_cause is: Predicted polar cyclones causing damaging winds. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 22nd August 2024 to at least 27th August 2024. Its affected region is: Southern half of Australia, starting from Western Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather-related. Its causes are: Polar cyclones causing strong wind yields across the NEM, potential for damaging winds and gales.

title: What the last twenty years of the ESOO tells us about blackout predictions
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "What the last twenty years of the ESOO tells us about blackout predictions" posted at 22-08-2024 01:13:00 PM is: The news article examines historical data from the Australian Energy Market Operator's (AEMO) Electricity Statement of Opportunities (ESOO) over the past two decades, discussing how the forecasts often predict supply shortfalls that do not ultimately occur. Despite updates in methodology and policy changes such as the Retailer Reliability Obligation and the Interim Reliability Measure, the ESOO's primary purpose remains to inform and elicit market responses rather than act as a direct predictor of blackouts. It highlights misconceptions about the ESOO being a "blackout predictor" and explains that while it projects supply challenges, the National Electricity Market (NEM) has maintained high reliability levels. The article suggests that the ESOO serves more as a market tool to prevent reliability issues rather than an accurate indicator of imminent blackouts; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term ('decade ahead' projections). Its root_cause is: Evolving energy system modeling and methodology changes. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 22-08-2024 01:13:00 PM. Its affected region is: Australia (National Electricity Market areas). Its affected key users are: Energy market stakeholders. Its cause type is: Forecast methodology and policy evolution. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Over-forecasting of demand, lack of model sophistication in the past, shifts to probabilistic forecasting methods, policy changes affecting reliability measures.

title: Callide C3 returns to service, a couple days earlier than schedule
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Callide C3 returns to service, a couple days earlier than schedule," posted at 22-08-2024 08:18:00 AM, is: The Callide C3 unit, which had been offline due to outages, including a significant 518-day break caused by a cooling tower failure, resumed operation earlier than anticipated on 21st August 2024. Initially expected to be back by 26th August, the early return of this unit is significant for stabilizing supply. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: medium-term. Its root_cause is: cooling tower failure. Its accident scale is: significant, affecting a major power unit with a lengthy downtime. Its dates are: 31st October 2022 (initial outage), 1st April 2024 (initial return to service), and 21st August 2024 (earlier-than-scheduled return). Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Energy markets relying on Callide C3 output. Its cause type is: technical/mechanical failure. Its causes are: cooling tower failure, lengthy repair process, and maintenance outages.

title: Morning burst of volatility in South Australia (Thursday 22nd August 2024)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Morning burst of volatility in South Australia (Thursday 22nd August 2024)" posted at 22-08-2024 07:52:00 AM is: The article reports a morning burst of volatility in South Australia's electricity market due to factors like a ramp in demand, constrained interconnectors, and low variable renewable energy (VRE) production, leading to a reduction in supply options. Such conditions are a common occurrence in the region. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate/short-term. Its root_cause is: Increased demand combined with constrained interconnectors and low VRE. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 22-08-2024. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market dynamics/weather conditions. title: Reviewing rebids at Loy Yang A1 for Tuesday 20th August 2024 … [alternate title = tips for ez2view users in the ‘Bid Explorer’ widget]
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Reviewing rebids at Loy Yang A1 for Tuesday 20th August 2024 … [alternate title = tips for ez2view users in the ‘Bid Explorer’ widget]" posted at 21-08-2024 07:59:00 PM is: The article discusses a series of rebids at the Loy Yang A1 unit due to an unplanned outage caused by an auxiliary plant failure. The outage resulted in multiple bid adjustments throughout the day as the plant's availability was affected, prompting changes to the bids in response to forecasted prices. This highlights the need to stabilize auxiliary systems to ensure reliability in coal-fired power generation. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate but ongoing until the unit is fully operational again. Its root_cause is: Auxiliary plant failure leading to a generator outage. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 20-08-2024 to 21-08-2024. Its affected region is: Loy Yang, Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity grid operators and consumers relying on power supply from the Loy Yang A1 unit. Its cause type is: Technical failure. Its causes are: Auxiliary plant failure, resulting in power generation disruption and multiple rebids by the operator in response to forecasted electricity market prices.

title: Using the ‘Next Day Public’ data set to see more about yesterday evening’s unplanned outage at Loy Yang A1 (which remains offline at this point)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Using the ‘Next Day Public’ data set to see more about yesterday evening’s unplanned outage at Loy Yang A1 (which remains offline at this point)" posted at 21-08-2024 10:37:00 AM is: An unplanned outage occurred at the Loy Yang A1 coal-fired power unit, which took the unit offline. The outage was noted at 16:25 on August 20, 2024, and it is expected to return online by August 25, 2024, as suggested by available data from the generator, AGL Energy. This incident caused concern among energy analysts and observers due to its potential impact on the electricity market, though it is not indicated that such outages are becoming more frequent.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). title: Millmerran unit 2 offline on unplanned outage – Wednesday 21st August 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Millmerran unit 2 offline on unplanned outage – Wednesday 21st August 2024" posted at 21-08-2024 10:08:00 AM is: The Millmerran Unit 2 experienced an unplanned outage, with notifications indicating the unit was offline shortly before 08:30. The incident was not a planned outage, as no updates were shown in the MT PASA DUID Availability data, and reasons for this outage will be more evident after the next data update. The event led to a minor drop in system frequency on the mainland, likely mitigated by Primary Frequency Response. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. The unit came offline unexpectedly this afternoon from a low load position, which led analysts to suspect the outage was not pre-planned, as typically outages occur from near full load. The incident did not cause a significant frequency drop, suggesting a smaller disturbance than past events. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). title: Tarong North Power Station trips on Monday evening 19th August 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Tarong North Power Station trips on Monday evening 19th August 2024" posted at 19-08-2024 06:47:00 PM is: The Tarong North Power Station experienced an unexpected outage on Monday evening, August 19, 2024. The station came offline from near full load, causing a noticeable frequency dip which was corrected shortly after through Contingency FCAS and Primary Frequency Response. The outage was unplanned and is classified as a forced outage with an unknown duration at this point. The actual capacity of the power station at the time of the outage was slightly lower than its maximum, raising questions about its recent operational status. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Forced outage of the power station. title: Watt scarcity?
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Watt scarcity?" posted at 19-08-2024 02:05:00 PM is: In late July and early August, the National Electricity Market (NEM) saw several instances of price volatility, particularly from 28 July to 6 August. The volatility was most pronounced in South Australia, influenced by interconnector limitations, while the whole NEM experienced significant spikes on 5 August. The post explores whether these price increases were due to supply scarcity or other factors, suggesting that the concentration of ownership among the "Big Four" generators likely played a role. The lack of generation scarcity, despite low wind contributions, points to contract positions of generators influencing bidding behavior. Specifically, generators may have higher uncontracted capacities offered at extreme prices, contributing to price spikes. This issue raises questions about market dynamics and whether structural changes are needed to address such outcomes. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term impact during late July to early August. Its root_cause is: Ownership concentration and contracting dynamics. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28 July 2024 to 6 August 2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), notably South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants and consumers. Its cause type is: Market operations and dynamics. Its causes are: Interconnector limitations, ownership concentration among key generators, low wind energy contributions, and bidding strategies influenced by contracting positions.

title: Queensland ‘Market Demand’ drops below 3,000MW (for the first time in over 20 years), on Sunday 18th August 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Queensland ‘Market Demand’ drops below 3,000MW (for the first time in over 20 years), on Sunday 18th August 2024" posted at 18-08-2024 01:07:00 PM is: The article highlights a significant reduction in the electricity demand in Queensland to 2,975MW on August 18, 2024, marking it as the lowest in over two decades. This drop in demand is associated with high production from rooftop photovoltaic (PV) systems, estimated at around 4,000MW at the time, which is challenging the electricity grid's operation. Past and recent demand fluctuations signify the ongoing challenges presented by increased rooftop solar adoption and the need for grid redesign to accommodate these changes. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing as rooftop PV installations continue to grow. Its root_cause is: Increased rooftop PV generation leading to reduced grid demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-08-2024; comparisons are made to prior instances such as 1st October 2023 and 22nd January 2024. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Electricity market stakeholders and utility companies. Its cause type is: Technological and market-driven. Its causes are: High rooftop PV output reducing grid electricity demand, continued growth of rooftop solar installations.

title: Wobbles in recent days, at Loy Yang A
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Wobbles in recent days, at Loy Yang A" posted at 15-08-2024 01:02:00 PM is: The recent outages at Loy Yang A coal-fired power station have seen three out of four units becoming offline, impacting electricity supply stability. This situation has disrupted system frequency, evidenced by a system frequency drop on August 14th when unit 4 tripped. The unit had returned online overnight but went offline again by early afternoon on August 15th; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to short-term. Its root cause is: Frequent trips of units at the Loy Yang A station. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: August 5th, 2024, to August 15th, 2024. Its affected region is: Loy Yang, Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational failures. Its causes are: multiple outages of the power units at Loy Yang A, including a trip on August 14th and subsequent offline status of the unit on August 15th.

title: AEMO commences publishing actual intermittent generators (SCADA) availability data to the EMMS Data Model
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO commences publishing actual intermittent generators (SCADA) availability data to the EMMS Data Model" posted at 14-08-2024 08:00:00 PM is: The article reports that the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has started publishing actual intermittent generators' (SCADA) availability data to the EMMS Data Model and NEMWeb, enhancing transparency in forecasting processes for semi-scheduled generation by making real-time data, such as turbine availability and meteorological conditions, accessible. This initiative is seen as a move towards improving the accuracy and reliability of power forecasting by addressing long-standing challenges related to data invisibility for semi-scheduled generators.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term improvement in forecast reliability. Its root_cause is: Enhancement of data transparency by AEMO. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 14-08-2024, 13-08-2024. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy market participants using the EMMS Model and NEMWeb. Its cause type is: Data transparency and availability improvement. Its causes are: Publishing of SCADA data for semi-scheduled generators by AEMO.

title: System frequency drop with trip at Loy Yang A4 on Wednesday 14th August 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "System frequency drop with trip at Loy Yang A4 on Wednesday 14th August 2024" posted at 14-08-2024 07:10:00 PM is: A brief article reports the unexpected trip of the Loy Yang A4 coal unit, impacting the electricity grid with a notable frequency drop. The event was recorded by the ez2view ‘Notifications’ widget during the 5-minute period ending at 17:51 on the same day. Despite this alert, it is emphasized that recent reports of coal unit outages do not indicate an increase in their frequency. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). title: Wet weather in QLD brings reduced solar output during August 12 and 13, 2024
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Wet weather in QLD brings reduced solar output during August 12 and 13, 2024" posted at 14-08-2024 04:12:00 PM is: The article discusses the significant impact of extreme rainfall on solar energy production in Queensland and northern New South Wales on August 12th and 13th, 2024. The heavy rains, which were reportedly the wettest in August on record for Rockhampton, led to a dramatic reduction in both large-scale and rooftop solar output in Queensland. Forecasts had anticipated the reduced solar availability 2.5 days in advance, with solar outputs nearing record lows for the winter season; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (from August 12-13, 2024, potentially extending slightly beyond as existing solar output deficits could affect market pricing or grid stability temporarily). Its root_cause is: Extreme wet weather and cloud cover. Its accident scale is: Regional (affecting a portion of Queensland and northern New South Wales). Its dates are: August 12-13, 2024. Its affected region is: Queensland and northern New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Solar energy producers, electricity grids reliant on solar. Its cause type is: Weather-induced. Its causes are: Heavy rainfall in the region, increased cloud cover, reduced sunlight for solar collection.

title: Spot price volatility (in SA only?) on Tuesday evening 6th August 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot price volatility (in SA only?) on Tuesday evening 6th August 2024" posted at 06-08-2024 06:36:00 PM is: The article reports on the spot price volatility in South Australia on Tuesday evening, with the price reaching just above $12,500/MWh at 18:25. It specifically notes three price excursions in South Australia around or above the $1,000/MWh threshold, occurring at 17:00, slightly below $1,000/MWh at 18:00, and at 18:25. There were no comparable price excursions in other regions, and the cumulative price in South Australia has decreased slightly from the previous evening due to the diminishing impact of volatility from about a week ago; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 06-08-2024. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market-driven price fluctuations. title: A look at storage levels at the Iona underground storage facility, August 2024
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "A look at storage levels at the Iona underground storage facility, August 2024" posted at 06-08-2024 02:32:00 PM is: The article discusses the critical role of firming electricity supply during the winter, particularly given the extended cold snap in July 2024, which drove high electricity demand in South Australia and beyond. This situation highlighted reliance on gas-powered generation (GPG) and storage systems, particularly Iona Underground Storage (UGS) in Victoria, which reported high withdrawal rates, including a peak of 432 TJ/d on July 17, 2024. The GSOO notes insufficient storage capacity during extreme weather conditions, necessitating proper storage management. Current storage at Iona stands at 10,134 TJ, with declines expected until refilling for winter 2025 begins. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Fuel supply issues (gas). Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Throughout Winter 2024. Its root_cause is: Extreme cold weather leading to increased electricity demand and reliance on gas storage. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 17-07-2024; ongoing effects into August 2024. Its affected region is: South Australia and the National Electricity Market (NEM) regions. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers reliant on firming capacity, particularly during peak demand. Its cause type is: Environmental and resource-related. Its causes are: Extended cold snap, high electricity demand, high gas withdrawal rates, and limited storage capacity.

title: Evening burst of volatility across the NEM on Monday 5th August 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Evening burst of volatility across the NEM on Monday 5th August 2024" posted on 05-08-2024 06:13:00 PM is: The article reports on a volatile evening in the National Electricity Market (NEM) on August 5th, 2024, driven by a tight supply-demand balance as the Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) dropped under 15%, triggering a 'yellow alert.' Additional factors include elevated cumulative prices, particularly in South Australia and climbing prices in New South Wales and Victoria, coupled with increased Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) costs. Contributing to this volatility are evening peak demand over 30,000 MW, low wind generation under 1,000 MW, reduced solar output due to cloud cover and evening timing, and the offline status of seven coal units, with Loy Yang A4's outage particularly significant. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term impact. Its root_cause is: Multiple factors, including demand peaks, low renewable generation, and generator outages. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-08-2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), with specific impacts in South Australia, New South Wales, and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and market participants in affected regions. Its cause type is: Systematic and operational. Its causes are: Tight supply-demand balance, low wind and solar generation, coal unit outages, and specific regional price trends.

title: With the benefit of hindsight – or who’d be an auto-bidder?
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "With the benefit of hindsight – or who’d be an auto-bidder?" posted at 05-08-2024 03:13:00 PM is: On July 30, 2024, South Australia experienced significant electricity market price volatility, with spot prices exceeding $1,000/MWh across 32 intervals due to interconnector limitations and specific bidding behaviors by generators. The Murraylink interconnector was out of service, significantly limiting the Heywood interconnector capacity, effectively isolating South Australia from the rest of the National Electricity Market. The limitations led to economic isolation, exacerbating price volatility. Although the battery fleet was initially well-utilized, a misalignment between the autobidder software projections and unfolding market conditions, combined with short storage durations, led to depleted energy reserves. This resulted in diminished revenue opportunities during a second intense price spike period. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term impact on July 30, 2024. Its root_cause is: Interconnector limitations between South Australia and Victoria combined with dynamic market bidding behavior. Its accident scale is: Local, specific to South Australia's electricity market. Its dates are: 30-07-2024. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity generators and battery storage operators in South Australia. Its cause type is: Infrastructure limitations and market behavior. Its causes are: Interconnector outages and limitations, dynamic autobidding behaviors, and limited battery storage capacity.

title: Morning burst of volatility across the NEM on Monday 5th August 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Morning burst of volatility across the NEM on Monday 5th August 2024" posted at 05-08-2024 09:38:00 AM is: The article reports a morning surge in electricity market volatility across all five regions of the National Electricity Market (NEM), though Queensland and Tasmania experienced less impact. Between 07:15 and 09:20, the prices fluctuated significantly but subsided afterward. Key contributing factors include a morning peak in demand, low wind output, reduced solar yield due to winter and cloud cover, and outage of seven out of 44 coal units, which collectively led to increased market demand and price instability. South Australia's cumulative price rise over $1,000,000 was particularly noted, indicating stress in the system.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term, throughout the morning. Its root cause is: Combination of high demand peak, low renewable output, and coal unit outages. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-08-2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market, with particular emphasis on South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in the NEM, particularly in South Australia. Its cause type is: Grid supply-demand imbalance. Its causes are: Morning peak in demand, low wind and solar output, coal unit outages.

title: A (very!) quick look at bidding behaviour (NEM-wide) over recent days
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A (very!) quick look at bidding behaviour (NEM-wide) over recent days" posted at 01-08-2024 02:12:00 PM is: The article discusses recent electricity market volatility observed across various regions in the National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly focusing on South Australia from July 28th to August 1st, 2024. The key issue highlighted is the significant bidding behavior by large energy players who took advantage of competitive power prices and market dynamics, following multiple coal plant failures to engage in deliberate bidding strategies. This led to higher prices despite increased availability of cheaper energy, driven largely by increased demand due to colder weather, impacting the market across different days. There's speculation about the recurring nature of these bidding patterns throughout the week, influencing ongoing and future market dynamics. Its relevance level is: Level 2; Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: July 28th to August 1st, 2024. Its root_cause is: Deliberate bidding behavior by large energy players exploiting market conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: July 28, 2024, to August 1, 2024. Its affected region is: South Australia and all regions in the NEM. Its affected key users are: Market participants and potentially consumers affected by high electricity prices. Its cause type is: Market dynamics and strategic bidding behavior. Its causes are: Coal plant failures, colder weather increasing demand, and deliberate bidding patterns by large energy players.

title: Callide C3 offline until expected return to service on Monday 26th August 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Callide C3 offline until expected return to service on Monday 26th August 2024" posted at 31-07-2024 07:54:00 PM is: The Callide C3 unit at the Callide Power Station went offline on 26th July 2024 due to a technical fault in its battery charger. CS Energy has initiated an investigation into the cause of the fault and plans to have the unit back in service by 26th August 2024. This issue has contributed to recent price volatility across the National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly in South Australia. No injuries occurred during the shutdown, and the affected equipment is still under warranty. The fault was identified as a rare component failure in the battery charger, and a rebuild and factory testing are underway. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Until 26th August 2024. Its root cause is: Technical fault in battery charger. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 26th July 2024 to 26th August 2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Equipment failure. Its causes are: Component failure in battery charger, resulting in unit shutdown.

author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Keep an eye on the Cumulative Price … especially in South Australia" posted at 31-07-2024 08:06:00 AM is: The article discusses recent volatility in the National Electricity Market (NEM), with particular emphasis on South Australia, where significant price fluctuations were observed on the evenings of July 28-30, 2024. title: Energy price volatility on 30th July 2024
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Energy price volatility on 30th July 2024" posted at 30-07-2024 05:51:00 PM is: The article discusses an episode of significant energy price spikes across the National Electricity Market (NEM) regions on July 30, 2024. South Australia (SA) was the first region affected, reaching prices of $3,936.60/MWh at 17:10. Constraints on the Heywood interconnector and an outage on the Moorabool – Mortlake No.2 500kV line contributed to the volatility. Prices sustained high levels with SA exceeding the market price cap of $17,500/MWh later in the evening. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) declared a Lack Of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) condition in SA due to insufficient capacity reserves, which persisted until 20:30. Prices in SA remained elevated throughout the evening, highlighting system strain. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate, impacting from afternoon till late evening on 30th July 2024. Its root cause is: Grid constraints, transmission line outage, and high demand with limited supply capacity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-07-2024. Its affected region is: South Australia, with some impact on New South Wales and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers in SA, particularly large industrial users. Its cause type is: System constraints, transmission failures, and high demand. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Heywood interconnector constraint, Moorabool – Mortlake No.2 500kV line outage, high market demand, insufficient reserve capacity.

author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "How rare are simultaneous price spikes in all five NEM regions, like what we saw last night?" posted at 30-07-2024 02:42:00 PM is: Last night saw a rare occurrence of simultaneous price spikes across all five NEM regions, with dispatch prices exceeding $1,000/MWh starting at 17:55 and peaking above $3,000/MWh during subsequent intervals. This price surge was influenced by high demand and low wind generation, compounded by unplanned outages in key thermal units. The event is notable due to the rare simultaneous nature of these spikes across the NEM, reminiscent of the 2022 energy crisis, and was driven by cold weather increasing demand and various supply constraints. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. title: Quarterly Reviews (for 2024 Q2) from AEMO and AER released same day … on Wednesday 24th July 2024.
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Quarterly Reviews (for 2024 Q2) from AEMO and AER released same day … on Wednesday 24th July 2024." posted at 27-07-2024 06:18:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) and Australian Energy Regulator (AER) released their quarterly reports for Q2 2024 simultaneously, highlighting significant trends in the National Electricity Market (NEM). During this period, colder weather on the east coast and warmer conditions on the west influenced generation patterns, with low winds and reduced rainfall impacting renewable energy output. Consequently, electricity production from wind and hydro decreased by 20% and 18% respectively, whereas gas and black coal generation saw increases due to higher demand and network outages. Electricity prices surged across all regions except Queensland, driven by a combination of these factors, as well as generator outages and lower renewable energy contributions. The reports underscore the growing importance of flexible gas-powered generation during periods of reduced renewable output and the need for adaptable strategies to stabilize the market. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Fuel supply issues (coal, gas). The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Q2 2024. The root_cause is: Low wind speeds, reduced rainfall, and increased demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 24-07-2024 (main) and other related dates include 17-07-2024 (release of a related AER report). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) regions in Australia, with variable impacts per region. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers and utility companies within the NEM. Its cause type is: Weather-related and infrastructure operational issues. Its causes are: Low wind speeds, reduced rainfall leading to decreased renewable output, increased demand, network outages, and a rise in gas and coal reliance.

The LOR1 condition is forecasted for the mornings of July 29th, 30th, and 31st, with reserve capacity potentially falling below requirements as market demand approaches all-time highs, compounded by reduced hydro storage levels and anticipated low wind generation. title: A tale of two mega-projects: Project Energy Connect and Snowy 2.0 timelines (Part 2)
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "A tale of two mega-projects: Project Energy Connect and Snowy 2.0 timelines (Part 2)" posted at 24-07-2024 02:05:00 PM is: The article discusses the ongoing challenges and timeline revisions faced by the Snowy 2.0 mega-project, highlighting its complexities and delays since being announced in 2017. The project, which aims to connect two dams from the original Snowy Mountains Scheme for a pumped hydro facility, has encountered geological challenges, cost overruns, and unexpected incidents, leading to a revised cost estimate of $12 billion and a new completion date of December 2028. These issues, common in large-scale projects, also reflect broader challenges in the National Electricity Market (NEM), emphasizing the underestimation of costs and the 'eternal beginner syndrome' in government-initiated projects. The report also references similar challenges in other large projects like Basslink and SunCable. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term, as the project's delays and cost overruns extend its expected benefits and affect investor confidence. Its root_cause is: Complex geological conditions, cost underestimation, and project management challenges. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 24-07-2024. Its affected region is: Snowy Mountains region, Australia. Its affected key users are: Consumers and investors in the NEM. Its cause type is: Project management and environmental challenges. title: Tamar Valley CCGT switches off on Tue 23rd July (after running for almost 7 weeks straight)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Tamar Valley CCGT switches off on Tue 23rd July (after running for almost 7 weeks straight)" posted at 24-07-2024 10:18:00 AM is: The news discusses the shutdown of the Tamar Valley Combined Cycle Gas Turbine (CCGT) on July 23, 2024, after operating continuously for nearly 7 weeks. The unit was initially planned to run until the end of July but was taken offline earlier due to an unscheduled trip, leading Hydro Tasmania to adjust the outage schedule. The planned outage was originally set to begin on July 28, but due to the trip, there might have been changes to the schedule, potentially starting the outage sooner. During its operation, the unit was running at its maximum capacity to meet electricity demand driven by cold-induced demand paired with low hydro and wind levels. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its affected region is: Tasmania (TAS), Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Tasmania relying on gas-fired power. Its cause type is: Technical failure leading to unit trip. title: Two news updates from CS Energy on Wed 17th July 2024 … the (final) Brady Report (about Callide C4), and the Hartz EPM Report (on the Cooling Towers)
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "Two news updates from CS Energy on Wed 17th July 2024 … the (final) Brady Report (about Callide C4), and the Hartz EPM Report (on the Cooling Towers)" posted at 17-07-2024 01:54:00 PM is: CS Energy released two significant reports addressing past failures and future safety plans. The Brady-Heywood Report details the explosive failure at Callide C4, and the Hartz EPM Report covers the cooling tower issues affecting Callide C3 and C4. These events caused significant demand loss and had long-term operational impacts on the Queensland electricity market. The Callide C3 unit resumed operation in April 2024, while Callide C4's service return is delayed until August 2024. CS Energy also announced a new safety and improvement plan to prevent future incidents; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures, grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Extended impact from May 2021 through at least August 2024 due to ongoing operational delays and incidents. Its root cause is: Explosive failure at Callide C4 and cooling tower collapse affecting Callide C3. Its accident scale is: Large-scale impact due to loss of ~2000MW of demand and extended outages. Its dates are: Main event on 25th May 2021; cooling tower collapse on 31st October 2022; partial resolution on 1st April 2024 and projected completion by 31st August 2024. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Queensland, particularly during the demand loss event. Its cause type is: Mechanical and structural failures. Its causes are: Callide C4 explosion and Callide C3 cooling tower collapse.

title: First operations of any BDU (Wandoan BESS) on Tuesday 16th July 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "First operations of any BDU (Wandoan BESS) on Tuesday 16th July 2024" posted at 16-07-2024 02:15:00 PM is: The Australian Clean Energy Council's summit coincided with the significant event of the Wandoan Battery in Queensland becoming the first to operate as a Bidirectional Unit (BDU) under the new Implementation of the IESS Rule Change. This transition marks a milestone in the integration of battery systems into the Australian Energy Market Operator's framework, indicating enhanced operational capabilities and regulatory adaptations. This change, observed through a specific ez2view configuration, follows new AEMO registration parameters for battery units. Updates to operational dashboards for BDU, including effective ramp rates and conformance obligations, were also noted. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing as the new market framework adapts. Its root_cause is: Implementation of the IESS Rule Change by AEMO. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-07-2024. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Energy market operators and participants. Its cause type is: Regulatory change. Its causes are: Implementation of the IESS Rule Change, operational registration update of battery systems, and introduction of BDUs in AEMO's framework.

title: A tale of two mega-projects: Project Energy Connect and Snowy 2.0 timelines (Part 1)
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "A tale of two mega-projects: Project Energy Connect and Snowy 2.0 timelines (Part 1)" posted at 16-07-2024 07:15:00 AM is: The news article examines the timeline and challenges faced by Project Energy Connect (PEC), an interconnector project between South Australia and New South Wales, paralleling issues faced by the Snowy 2.0 project. The PEC, originally planned as 'Riverlink' in 1997, has undergone various phases, from a feasibility study in 2016 to multiple changes in cost and completion date estimates. Delays and increased expenses due to logistical issues, raw material costs, and COVID-19-related setbacks have led to completion estimates shifting from mid-2023 to July 2027. Despite the significant market benefits projected from the interconnector, skepticism surrounds the cost and timeline predictions for such mega-projects; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium to long term, delayed effects. Its root_cause is: Logistical and economic challenges, exacerbated by COVID-19-related issues and contractual defaults. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-07-2024. Its affected region is: South Australia, New South Wales. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Construction and economic delays. Its causes are: COVID-19 execution delays, cost of raw materials, contractual defaults.

title: Preliminary Report Released: NSW Load Shedding on 8 July 2024
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Preliminary Report Released: NSW Load Shedding on 8 July 2024" posted at 15-07-2024 03:49:00 PM is: The preliminary report on the load shedding incident in New South Wales on 8 July 2024 reveals a sequence of 18 events contributing to the occurrence. Unplanned network outages led the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) to direct load shedding, which affected 40 MW in total, with initial shedding of 30 MW at 18:17 and another 10 MW at 18:55 in the Lismore area. The incident involved complex coordination between AEMO, Transgrid, APA Group, and Essential Energy, with further analysis pending for a detailed final report.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term, affecting the immediate period during and possibly days following the event. The root cause is: Unplanned network outages. The accident scale is: Regional. The dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 8 July 2024 and 9 July 2024. The affected region is: Lismore area, New South Wales. The affected key users are: Unknown. The cause type is: Technical/operational failure. The causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Unplanned network outages causing load shedding to manage electricity supply.

title: One more delay to Return-to-Service for Callide C4 … another 40 days added to the outage
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "One more delay to Return-to-Service for Callide C4 … another 40 days added to the outage" posted at 12-07-2024 06:46:00 PM is: The return-to-service date for the Callide C4 unit has been delayed again, now scheduled for 31st August 2024, due to additional time needed for testing and quality assurance. This extends a previously anticipated return date of 22nd July 2024. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, up to the end of August 2024. Its root_cause is: Delay in repair completion and need for further testing and quality assurance. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: from 22nd July 2024 to 31st August 2024. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Technical/operational. Its causes are: Delay in testing and quality assurance processes leading to postponement of the service return date.

title: Forecast evening IRPM next week healthy … despite ‘ferocious Tasman Low’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Forecast evening IRPM next week healthy … despite ‘ferocious Tasman Low’" posted at 12-07-2024 09:33:00 AM is: The article discusses a forecast of a 'ferocious Tasman Low' expected to affect southeast Australia, emphasizing its potential impact on the National Electricity Market (NEM), which is sensitive to weather conditions. Despite the cold weather and high offshore winds from the Tasman Low, the NEM is predicted to maintain a stable supply margin, with the Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) staying above 20% at the evening peak on most days in the coming week. Concerns exist about gas storage and production levels, but the forecast indicates less strain on the system compared to previous weeks in July. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Next week. Its root cause is: Ferocious Tasman Low weather system. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 15th July - 18th July 2024. Its affected region is: Southeast Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather-driven. Its causes are: Forecasted cold weather, high winds from Tasman Low, concerns about gas storage and production levels.

title: Tamar Valley CCGT clocks over 5 weeks of almost continuous operation at full load in Winter 2024 … but hydro storages still not great
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Tamar Valley CCGT clocks over 5 weeks of almost continuous operation at full load in Winter 2024 … but hydro storages still not great" posted at 11-07-2024 02:38:00 PM is: The Tamar Valley combined-cycle gas turbine (CCGT) in Tasmania has been operating continuously at full load (208MW) for over five weeks since resuming its operations on June 6, 2024, after a five-year hiatus. This operation has been crucial to the energy supply due to low hydro storage levels, which are currently at 31% despite increased gas peaking and contributions from wind farms and inter-state imports via Basslink. The situation highlights ongoing challenges in managing energy reserves while ensuring stability and reliability of the regional power supply; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Fuel supply issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing since June 6, 2024, with potential implications for the upcoming months. Its root_cause is: Increased reliance on gas generation due to insufficient hydroelectric storage levels. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-07-2024. Its affected region is: Tasmania. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational and supply constraint. Its causes are: Prolonged low hydro storage levels, increased demand for stable energy supply, and reliance on gas due to insufficient hydroelectric generation.

title: Final figures for wind production through 2024 Q2
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Final figures for wind production through 2024 Q2" posted at 09-07-2024 06:08:00 PM is: The article reports on the unusually low wind production levels during the second quarter of 2024 across the National Electricity Market (NEM), marking the worst quarter since Q2 of 2017 despite increased wind capacity. Various factors such as persistent blocking high-pressure systems, potential plant unavailability, and spillage due to economic reasons or network constraints are discussed as contributors to this low production. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: 2024 Q2. Its root cause is: Reduced wind resource availability and other operational factors. Its accident scale is: N/A (No specific accident reported). Its dates are: Same as main date (09-07-2024). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Electricity suppliers and consumers relying on wind energy. Its cause type is: Multiple contributing factors. Its causes are: Low wind availability, potential wind farm unavailability, economic curtailment, and network constraints leading to spillage.

title: AEMO directs load shedding (following unplanned network outages) in NSW on Monday evening 8th July 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO directs load shedding (following unplanned network outages) in NSW on Monday evening 8th July 2024" posted at 09-07-2024 05:52:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) directed load shedding in New South Wales on the evening of July 8, 2024, to maintain power system security following an unplanned network outage. This action affected around 24,000 customers in northern NSW, primarily in the region around Byron Bay. The outage was related to multiple transmission line trips, including the tripping of Transgrid’s 330 kV Lismore to Coffs Harbour line, which necessitated the shedding of 40 megawatts of load. The load shedding started just after 6pm and was subsequently restored around 7:30pm, with completion by 10pm.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, as it required load shedding and could influence short-term electricity prices. Its root_cause is: Unplanned network outages and transmission line trips. Its accident scale is: Regional, affecting customers in northern NSW, notably around Byron Bay, Lismore, and Ballina. Its dates are: 08-07-2024 (main date and all relevant actions occurred on this date). Its affected region is: Northern New South Wales, particularly the area around Byron Bay. Its affected key users are: About 24,000 residential and possibly some commercial customers. Its cause type is: Infrastructure failure. Its causes are: Unplanned unavailability of multiple network components, including the tripping of key transmission lines.

title: Barron Gorge Power Station returns to service approximately six months after significant damage from Cyclone Jasper
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Barron Gorge Power Station returns to service approximately six months after significant damage from Cyclone Jasper" posted at 08-07-2024 11:25:00 AM is: CleanCo announced the return of the 66MW Barron Gorge Power Station, after a six-month repair due to damage from Cyclone Jasper in December 2023. The cyclone caused erosion and damage to the river’s earthen embankment, requiring reconstruction. By July 6th, 2024, both units at the station started ramping up operations and were fully operational by Sunday. The return was largely on schedule as initial forecasts had set a return date of July 1st, 2024. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Approximately six months. Its root_cause is: Damage from Cyclone Jasper. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 2023-12-20 to 2024-07-06. Its affected region is: Barron Gorge. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural disaster. Its causes are: Significant damage from Cyclone Jasper, erosion, embankment damage.

title: Unusual wind direction to elevate wind power
author: Ashleigh Madden
classified_content: The summary of the news "Unusual wind direction to elevate wind power" posted at 05-07-2024 04:05:00 PM is: After a period of low wind power generation due to a high-pressure system over the Bight, an unusual easterly wind surge is expected to temporarily elevate wind power across Queensland, New South Wales, and South Australia. This rare wind pattern will slightly increase wind farm output in the National Electricity Market (NEM), with conditions anticipated to be low to medium. The easterly winds, while not as strong as those from cold fronts, signify a temporary deviation from usual wind patterns, coinciding with a cloudband that may decrease solar output in the affected regions. Wind power is expected to improve significantly later next week with an advancing strong cold front. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (spanning the days from the event on July 5 through to its conclusion by July 12). Its root_cause is: An unusual and rare surge of easterly winds. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-07-2024 for publication, with wind changes noted from 04-07-2024 to 12-07-2024. Its affected region is: Queensland, New South Wales, South Australia, with potential implications for Victoria. Its affected key users are: Unknown, but generally energy consumers including businesses and residential users in the categories served by NEM. Its cause type is: Weather pattern anomaly. Its causes are: High-pressure system in the Bight, easterly wind surge, and associated cloudband affecting solar output.

title: A bounce back: a review of Q2 2024 prices
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "A bounce back: a review of Q2 2024 prices" posted at 04-07-2024 11:10:00 PM is: The analysis of Q2 2024 prices in the National Electricity Market (NEM) shows a notable increase in time-weighted average prices across four of the five regions, with Queensland being the exception where prices decreased. Significant price drivers included wind lulls, scheduled network outages, gas market issues like the Longford outage, and dry weather affecting hydro production. These factors contributed to a "bounce back" in electricity prices in comparison to Q2 2023, continuing the long-term trend of increased price volatility. The impact varied by region, with NSW experiencing several price spikes due to network constraints, while SA saw a sharp price elevation due to interconnector limits and low wind conditions; Tasmania faced dry conditions leading to high reliance on gas-fired generation. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: fuel supply issues (coal, gas). Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Q2 2024. Its root_cause is: Supply-demand pressures from adverse weather events and fuel supply constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 04-07-2024. Its affected region is: NEM regions including QLD, NSW, VIC, SA, TAS, and WA. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and generators within the NEM and WEM. Its cause type is: Multiple overlapping market and environmental factors. Its causes are: Wind lulls, network outages, gas market disturbances, dry weather conditions.

title: ABC report that the highest air pressure ever recorded in Australia was observed this morning, and likely to peak again this Saturday
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "ABC report that the highest air pressure ever recorded in Australia was observed this morning, and likely to peak again this Saturday" posted at 04-07-2024 04:49:00 PM is: The ABC reported a significant meteorological event in Tasmania, where a high-pressure system reached a near-record air pressure of 1,043.9 hPa, causing colder temperatures and wind lulls in the region. This system is expected to persist for several days, influencing weather patterns such as light winds and dry air. The Bureau of Meteorology confirmed a slight dip in the initial report, adjusting the highest pressure to 1,043.9 hPa, thus not breaking the 1967 record. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term impact over the next few days. Its root_cause is: Unusually high-pressure system. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 04-07-2024. Its affected region is: Tasmania and south-east Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its cause type is: Natural causes. Its causes are: High-pressure system leading to colder temperatures, wind lulls, and dry air affecting the energy system.

title: First Bidirectional Units on 4th July 2024
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "First Bidirectional Units on 4th July 2024" posted at 04-07-2024 08:18:00 AM is: On July 4, 2024, the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) saw the implementation of its first bidirectional units (BDUs) aimed at improving the integration of energy storage systems. This change marks a transition from the traditional two-identifier system for battery operations to a streamlined single identifier, offering ease in shifting between consumption and production modes. The initial BDUs include AGL's Broken Hill Battery in NSW and Wandoan Battery in QLD, marking a significant step towards operational efficiency in the NEM. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium-term (several weeks following the implementation). Its root_cause is: Implementation of 2024-06 IESS Rule Change. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 04-07-2024 (same as the main date). Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD), Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy storage operators in the Australian NEM. Its cause type is: Policy change. Its causes are: Introduction of bidirectional units to streamline battery operations and improve energy storage integration in the NEM.

title: A running view of the recent wind drought
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "A running view of the recent wind drought" posted at 26-06-2024 03:50:00 PM is: The article discusses the ongoing 'wind drought' affecting Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM) since April 2024, highlighting several instances of supply-demand tightness caused by prolonged wind lulls in the second quarter (Q2). These wind lulls have led to increased production from coal and decreased production from hydroelectric sources, contributing to higher emissions compared to previous years. Additionally, the article notes related challenges in the gas system and storage due to structural issues in production and declining storage levels at the Iona Underground Gas Facility. A visualization of wind generation trends reveals four distinct lulls between April and June 2024, with specific dates and regions affected, emphasizing the importance of wind resource diversity in the transition to renewable energy.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Throughout Q2 2024. Its root_cause is: Prolonged wind lulls and structural issues in gas production and storage. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13th April to 20th April, 13th May to 18th May, 22nd May to 28th May, 5th June to 11th June. Its affected region is: Victoria, New South Wales, South Australia, Tasmania, Queensland. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Environmental and infrastructure-related. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Wind lulls, increased coal and decreased hydro production, gas system issues, declining gas storage levels.

title: A similar trended view of the aggregate production from Coal-Fired Generation across the NEM through 2024 Q2
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A similar trended view of the aggregate production from Coal-Fired Generation across the NEM through 2024 Q2" posted at 26-06-2024 12:33:00 PM is: The article examines the trends in electricity production from coal-fired generators within the National Electricity Market (NEM) in the second quarter of 2024. The analysis shows that production volumes for 2024 are 3TWh-6TWh lower compared to 2015-2019 but have recovered compared to recent years, particularly from 2022, with a reported increase of 1.5TWh-2TWh. The increase in coal-fired generation partially offset the declines in wind and hydroelectric energy production. Key contributing factors include prior negative periods impacted by unexpected power station closures and low renewables performance. Overall, coal-fired generators have maintained relatively stable output despite forced outages. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: coal-fired policies. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: The impact is ongoing through Q2 2024, with comparisons made across a decade. Its root cause is: A combination of prior closures of significant power stations and variability in renewable and gas generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 26-06-2024. Its affected region is: The National Electricity Market (NEM) regions. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers reliant on stable electricity supply across NEM. Its cause type is: Supply-side dynamics. Its causes are: Closure of Liddell and Hazelwood Power Stations, unstable renewable energy outputs, and historical low coal unit availability.

title: AEMO releases the 2024 ISP
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO releases the 2024 ISP" posted at 26-06-2024 07:30:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has released its 2024 Integrated System Plan (ISP), which outlines the roadmap for the nation's electricity grid transformation. The report emphasizes the need for increased transmission infrastructure and addresses the anticipated challenges related to renewable energy scaling and coal exit strategies. It has sparked discussions across various media platforms and is expected to fuel further political debate about Australia's energy future. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Unknown. Its root cause is: Publication of AEMO's 2024 Integrated System Plan. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 26-06-2024. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Policy release. Its causes are: Need for more transmission, renewable energy scaling issues, coal exit strategies.

title: A similar trended view of the aggregate production from Gas-Fired Generation across the NEM through 2024 Q2
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A similar trended view of the aggregate production from Gas-Fired Generation across the NEM through 2024 Q2" posted at 25-06-2024 03:37:00 PM is: The article discusses the trends in gas-fired generation (GPG) across the National Electricity Market (NEM) for the second quarter of 2024. It highlights the impact of low production levels of other energy sources, such as wind and hydro, on the increased reliance on gas-fired generation, particularly in June 2024. The article also refers to concerns raised by the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) regarding the East Coast gas system constraints, affecting both upstream and downstream gas usage. These constraints are partly due to increased domestic heating demand and possible interruptions affecting major energy users. The gas-fired generation in June 2024 shows a usage pattern similar to that of June 2022 but markedly higher than June 2023. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Fuel supply issues (gas). Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to short-term (Q2 2024). Its root cause is: Constraints in the East Coast gas system and increased demand for gas-fired generation due to low wind and hydro production. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 22nd June 2024 - 25th June 2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users are: Major energy users and regions relying on gas supply. Its cause type is: System constraint and demand increase. Its causes are: Low production levels of wind and hydro energy, increased demand for domestic heating, and concerns over gas supply interruptions.

title: CS Energy releases most recent draft of the ‘Brady Report’
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "CS Energy releases most recent draft of the ‘Brady Report’" posted at 25-06-2024 11:30:00 AM is: The latest draft of the ‘Brady Report’ by CS Energy has been released, shedding light on the events surrounding the explosion, fire, and subsequent outage at Callide C4 on May 25, 2021. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term implications since the 2021 event. Its root_cause is: Generator failure leading to explosion and fire. Its accident scale is: Major, affecting a significant power facility. Its dates are: 25-06-2024 and 25-05-2021.
