Its causes are: Explosion at Callide C4 due to a generator failure leading to a prolonged outage and investigation.

title: Solar Farms in south-western NSW constrained to 0MW on Monday 24th June 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Solar Farms in south-western NSW constrained to 0MW on Monday 24th June 2024" posted at 24-06-2024 01:04:00 PM is: This article discusses a significant constraint set, 'N-DPWG_63_X5', affecting solar farms in south-western NSW, resulting in their output being reduced to 0MW for an entire day. The article suggests possible causes such as SCADA issues or control system actions but does not confirm the exact reason. These constraints led to a day-long outage of solar farms and some batteries until 17:00 on the same day. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate and same-day impact. Its root cause is: Unknown SCADA or control system issue. Its accident scale is: Regional. Its dates are: 24-06-2024. Its affected region is: South-western NSW. Its affected key users are: Solar farm operators and the broader electricity grid network. Its cause type is: Operational/Technical. Its causes are: Invocation of constraint set 'N-DPWG_63_X5', potential SCADA or control system action.

title: A similar trended view of the aggregate production from Hydro Discharge across the NEM through 2024 Q2
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A similar trended view of the aggregate production from Hydro Discharge across the NEM through 2024 Q2" posted at 23-06-2024 08:54:00 PM is: The article analyzes hydroelectric production for the second quarter of 2024 across the National Electricity Market, comparing it to nine other years, including periods affected by the 'Millennium Drought' and 'Carbon Tax.' It highlights that 2024 Q2, if trends continue, may see the second-lowest production among the selected years, driven by factors such as low hydro storage in Tasmania and the offline status of the Barron Gorge hydro due to prior damage. These conditions, coupled with market prices, may be impacting power generation schedules, drawing attention to the broader implications for electricity supply in the region.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Q2 2024. Its root_cause is: low hydroelectric production and unsuitable weather conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 23-06-2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market, focusing on Tasmania and Barron Gorge. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Environmental and operational. Its causes is: low levels of hydro storage in Tasmania, Barron Gorge hydro offline since December 2023, unclear impact on Snowy Hydro from NSW rainfall, limited time-shifting opportunities due to price performance.

title: Where’s the wind gone, through 2024 Q2?   … with June perhaps even worse than April or May!
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Where’s the wind gone, through 2024 Q2? … with June perhaps even worse than April or May!" posted on 22-06-2024 07:46:00 PM is: The news article discusses the significantly low wind energy output across the National Electricity Market (NEM) during the second quarter of 2024, marking it as the worst yield compared to the same period in the last five years despite increased wind farm capacity. The prolonged low wind conditions, influenced by persistent blocking high-pressure systems, are highlighted as a potential precursor to an emerging 2024 energy crisis. The news also examines the potential for wind farm plant unavailability, economic curtailment, and network constraints contributing to the suppressed wind power output. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: 2024 Q2 through 2024 Q3. Its root cause is: Low wind energy output due to prolonged lulls in wind resource. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 22-06-2024 to 30-06-2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) area. Its affected key users are: NEM electricity consumers and wind energy producers. Its cause type is: Meteorological and operational factors. Its causes are: Prolonged low wind conditions, possible plant unavailability, economic curtailment, and network constraints.

title: Forecast for a moderate burst of wind to round out the month or June 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Forecast for a moderate burst of wind to round out the month or June 2024" posted at 22-06-2024 03:31:00 PM is: The article discusses a moderate increase in wind energy generation forecasted by AEMO towards the end of June 2024. This forecast predicts a burst in wind energy output reaching approximately 5,500MW, expected to span a 24-hour period between Tuesday, June 25th and Wednesday, June 26th, 2024. This potential increase follows a recent trend of low wind energy yield noted throughout April and May, with an exception on May 30th when an all-time maximum was reached. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: June 25th to June 26th, 2024. Its root_cause is: Forecasted change in wind speed and yield. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 22-06-2024, with the predicted event from 25-06-2024 to 26-06-2024. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural variation. Its causes are: Low wind energy generation in previous months with an expected moderate burst of wind energy output.

title: Trended bids (and production) from gas-fired generation over the past 90 days
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Trended bids (and production) from gas-fired generation over the past 90 days" posted at 22-06-2024 12:17:00 PM is: The article provides an analysis of gas-fired generation in the National Electricity Market (NEM) over the past 90 days, noting a decrease in low-priced bids and an increase in production, particularly during peak evening times due to lower Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) conditions between June 17th and June 20th, with a temporary reprieve on June 21st. Current gas prices remain high. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Fuel prices. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate past 90 days moving forward. Its root cause is: Escalating gas-fired production and associated pricing. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: June 17th to June 21st, 2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Economic/Market-driven. Its causes are: Decreased volume of low-priced gas-fired generation bids, increased peak production during low IRPM periods.

title: Kogan Creek back online, late Friday 21st June 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Kogan Creek back online, late Friday 21st June 2024" posted at 22-06-2024 12:13:00 PM is: The Kogan Creek power station, which went offline on the morning of Thursday, 20th June 2024, has resumed operations as of Friday evening, 21st June 2024. This restoration is evidenced by adjustments in the 'Bids & Offers' widget in ez2view, captured on the 22nd of June. The facility was offline for approximately 36 hours. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term. Its root cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 22-06-2024. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational issue. Its causes are: Generator outage.

title: Tarong unit 3 comes offline late Friday afternoon 21st June 2024 – appears not planned in advance
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Tarong unit 3 comes offline late Friday afternoon 21st June 2024 – appears not planned in advance" posted at 21-06-2024 07:25:00 PM is: The article reports an unplanned outage of Tarong Unit 3, a coal-fired power plant, which went offline just before 16:40 on 21st June 2024. Given the heightened scrutiny on coal units in the context of the potential Energy Crisis of 2024, this incident received particular attention. At the time of reporting, there was no information available to suggest that the outage was planned or to predict when the unit might return online. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Unplanned outage of Tarong Unit 3. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 21-06-2024. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Equipment failure. Its causes are: Unplanned outage of Tarong Unit 3, Equipment failure at a coal-fired power plant.

title: Callide C4 return to service pushed back 3 weeks, now slated for late July 2024
author: Dan Lee
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "Callide C4 return to service pushed back 3 weeks, now slated for late July 2024" posted at 21-06-2024 04:30:00 PM is: The return to service (RTS) of Callide Unit C4, offline since May 25, 2021, due to an explosion, has been delayed by approximately three weeks, with partial capacity now expected on July 22, 2024, and full capacity on July 31, 2024. CS Energy CEO Darren Busine cited the delay was due to the availability of overseas specialists and extra testing time to manage emergent work during recommissioning, independent of the cooling tower rebuild timeline. The safety measures were highlighted as a priority even if dates are provisional. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/Generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Mid-July to end of July 2024. Its root_cause is: Delay in the availability of specialists and emergent work during recommissioning. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25th May 2021 to 31st July 2024. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational delays and logistical issues. Its causes are: Delay due to lack of specialist availability, extra testing time, and emergent recommissioning work.

title: Barron Gorge Unit 2 gets a run, on Friday 21st June 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Barron Gorge Unit 2 gets a run, on Friday 21st June 2024" posted at 21-06-2024 01:28:00 PM is: Barron Gorge Unit 2 has resumed operations following significant damage incurred in December 2023 due to Tropical Cyclone Jasper. A recent alert notified that the unit, along with Unit 1, has undergone a series of test runs in June 2024, indicating progress towards full operational capacity. The generator outage widget and bids indicate that both units are expected to return to full capacity of 33MW per unit by July 1, 2024.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium-term (until operational restoration). Its root_cause is: Damage from Tropical Cyclone Jasper. Its accident scale is: Localized to the Barron Gorge station. Its dates are: 21-06-2024, with previous activity on 12-06-2024. Its affected region is: Barron Gorge region. Its affected key users are: Regional electricity consumers and stakeholders relying on Barron Gorge station. Its cause type is: Natural disaster. Its causes are: Tropical Cyclone Jasper, repair and operational testing activities.

title: 190 minutes of low IRPM (reaching 11.01% at 18:05) on Thursday evening 20th June 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "190 minutes of low IRPM (reaching 11.01% at 18:05) on Thursday evening 20th June 2024" posted at 20-06-2024 07:02:00 PM is: The article discusses a significant event on June 20, 2024, where the electricity market's Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) reached a low point of 11.01% at 18:05, during a high market demand period with low wind output and 15% of coal units offline. Gas supply issues exacerbated the situation, maintaining the IRPM below 15% from 17:20 to 20:30. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather, turbine/generator failures, grid/system instability, fuel supply issues (coal, gas), coal-fired policies, gas pipeline failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Low wind output, coal units offline, and gas supply issues. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 20-06-2024. Its affected region is: NEM-wide (National Electricity Market in Australia). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in the NEM region. Its cause type is: Multiple contributing factors. Its causes are: Low wind output, 15% of coal units offline, gas supply issues, and high market demand.

title: A look at where the wind has and hasn’t been blowing over the last three nights
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "A look at where the wind has and hasn’t been blowing over the last three nights" posted at 20-06-2024 03:58:00 PM is: Over the past three nights, the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia has experienced supply-demand tightness due to a combination of cold temperatures, low wind speeds, and offline coal units, with the latter affecting the evening peaks. The Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) dropped below 15%, hitting a low of 10.89%. This situation led to minor price spikes in South Australia. Weather systems over eastern Australia contributed to low wind speeds, specifically in regions with high wind farm concentrations, like eastern South Australia and western Victoria. Limited wind generation was observed in Tasmania, Victoria, South Australia, and New South Wales, with slightly better conditions in southern Queensland on Monday evening. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (during the three nights mentioned and potentially extending through the week if conditions persist). Its root_cause is: Low wind speeds and offline coal units alongside cold weather increasing demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 2024-06-17 to 2024-06-19. Its affected region is: Eastern Australia including Tasmania, Victoria, South Australia, New South Wales, and Southern Queensland. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers in the NEM, particularly in regions with low wind generation capacity. Its cause type is: Combination of natural and technical factors. Its causes are: Cold temperatures increasing demand, low evening wind speeds, offline coal units contributing to reduced IRPM.

title: Kogan Creek comes offline, on Thursday 20th June 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Kogan Creek comes offline, on Thursday 20th June 2024" posted at 20-06-2024 03:51:00 PM is: The Kogan Creek power station went offline on the morning of June 20, 2024, as noted by the SCADA snapshot at 07:35, affecting its availability at a crucial dispatch interval. Data from the AEMO and various widgets in ez2view indicate that the issue may stem from a technical challenge, as suggested by recent bids referencing 'Unit Commitment' and 'unit offline revised'. CS Energy had anticipated reduced availability until July 1, 2024, although it seems hopeful the unit might resume operations before June 23. Despite prior indications and bid revisions, the exact cause behind the unit going offline remains unknown, pending access to CS Energy's internal logs.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown, possibly until July 1, 2024, but potentially resuming before June 23, 2024. Its root_cause is: Unknown, potential technical failure. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 20-06-2024. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Technical. Its causes are: Technical issue causing the Kogan Creek power station to unexpectedly go offline, necessitating bid modifications by CS Energy and adjustments in power availability forecasts by AEMO.

title: A brief review of the (only!) 88MW of wind output NEM-wide at 13:20 on Thursday 13th June 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A brief review of the (only!) 88MW of wind output NEM-wide at 13:20 on Thursday 13th June 2024" posted at 20-06-2024 01:01:00 PM is: The article reviews a particularly low aggregate wind output across the National Electricity Market (NEM) on June 13, 2024, where wind farms generated just 88MW, representing an instantaneous capacity factor of 0.77%. This low output was primarily contributed by South Australia, with other regions like Tasmania, Victoria, New South Wales, and Queensland experiencing very low wind speeds leading to this minimal output. No curtailment affected the wind output; it was simply because of the calm weather conditions. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market: Short-term (particularly the afternoon of the 13th of June 2024). Its root_cause is: Very low wind speeds across the NEM. Its accident scale is: No accident, just low wind condition. Its dates is: 13-06-2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market, with specific mentions of South Australia, Tasmania, Victoria, New South Wales, and Queensland. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers dependent on wind energy in the NEM. Its cause type is: Natural climatic conditions. Its causes is: Becalmed (very low wind) conditions across much of the NEM.

title: Correlation between low wind Capacity Factor and low IRPM (NEM-wide) in recent evenings + Thursday evening looks likely to be low, as well.
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Correlation between low wind Capacity Factor and low IRPM (NEM-wide) in recent evenings + Thursday evening looks likely to be low, as well." posted at 20-06-2024 10:46:00 AM is: The electricity market has recently experienced low Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) coinciding with low wind capacity factors. This situation emerged across several evenings, primarily from June 17th to June 19th, 2024, with forecasts suggesting a potential continuation on June 20th. Factors contributing to these conditions include some offline coal units and a stressed gas supply system, affecting both domestic heating and gas-powered generation. Such conditions highlight challenges in energy transition pathways, particularly those reliant on variable renewable energy (VRE) complemented by firming capacity. Notable events include the dramatic wind output variations, such as the trio of high-output days at the end of May and early June with record-setting levels, versus recent disappointing outputs. The need for significant firming capacity is underscored by ongoing requirements exceeding 30,000MW. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing. Its root_cause is: Low wind capacity factor combined with offline coal units. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 17-06-2024, 18-06-2024, 19-06-2024, forecast for 20-06-2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) regions. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers, both residential and industrial, particularly in southern regions relying on gas supply. Its cause type is: Supply-side constraints and variability. Its causes are: Offline coal units, low wind capacity, stressed gas supply system.

title: AEMO publishes ‘East Coast Gas System Risk or Threat Notice’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO publishes ‘East Coast Gas System Risk or Threat Notice’" posted at 20-06-2024 05:55:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued a Risk or Threat Notice for the East Coast Gas System due to potential gas supply shortfalls in the southern jurisdictions, effective from 19 June 2024, potentially lasting until 30 September 2024. This notice highlights concerns over depleted storage inventories at Iona UGS, constrained production at Longford Gas Plant due to an unplanned outage, and ongoing maintenance, coupled with high winter demand and gas-powered generation needs. The situation poses a risk to gas supply adequacy in the regions of NSW, ACT, VIC, SA, and TAS, particularly during peak winter demand. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Fuel supply issues (coal, gas). Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 19 June 2024 to no later than 30 September 2024. Its root_cause is: Depletion of southern storage inventories and constrained Longford production capacity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 19 June 2024 to 30 September 2024. Its affected region is: Southern jurisdictions (NSW, ACT, VIC, SA, TAS). Its affected key users are: Gas-powered generation and residential/commercial users in the affected regions. Its cause type is: Supply-side constraints and high demand factors. Its causes are: Depletion of storage inventories, Longford plant unplanned outage and maintenance, high gas-powered generation demand, lower than forecasted Longford production, continued storage inventory reduction.

title: A shorter and shallower bout of low IRPM on Wednesday evening 19th June 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A shorter and shallower bout of low IRPM on Wednesday evening 19th June 2024," posted at 19-06-2024 07:50:00 PM is: The article reports on a significant drop in the Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) to below 15% for most dispatch intervals on the evening of June 19, 2024, though not as severe or prolonged as on the previous two days. At the peak, market demand reached 32,262 MW, with wind energy contributing nearly 2,000 MW. Despite the tight supply-demand conditions, prices remained stable due to the absence of intra-regional transmission constraints. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term, affecting Wednesday evening. Its root cause is: Low reserve margins and high market demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 19-06-2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) area. Its affected key users are: Energy market participants and consumers. Its cause type is: System and demand dynamics. Its causes are: Low IRPM, high market demand, constrained interconnectors, relatively low solar and wind generation compared to demand.

title: Tomorrow’s brief network outage (on the Rowville – South Morang 500 kV line) *might* have high impact
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Tomorrow’s brief network outage (on the Rowville – South Morang 500 kV line) *might* have high impact" posted at 19-06-2024 01:18:00 PM is: The article discusses a planned network outage of the Rowville – South Morang 500 kV line in Victoria (VIC) region, scheduled for 20/06/2024 from 2:00 PM to 4:30 PM. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) warns that a credible contingency event during this outage could lead to a significant reduction in electricity generation in VIC, and/or restrict power transfer across the VIC – South Australia (SA) interconnector. The notification prompts interest due to potential impacts and limited frequency of such notices. The 'V-ROSM' constraint set, relevant to this outage, involves multiple constraint equations that manage the electricity flow, highlighting the importance of monitoring the event.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 20/06/2024 from 2:00 PM to 4:30 PM. Its root_cause is: Planned maintenance of the transmission line. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 19/06/2024 (announcement) and 20/06/2024 (event). Its affected region is: Victoria (VIC) region, potentially affecting South Australia (SA) via the interconnector. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Planned outage. Its causes is: Maintenance activities on the transmission line, potential reduction in generation, potential restriction on the VIC-SA interconnector.

title: NEM-wide ‘Market Demand’ above 30,000MW on Wednesday *morning* 19th June 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM-wide ‘Market Demand’ above 30,000MW on Wednesday morning 19th June 2024" posted at 19-06-2024 08:05:00 AM is: On the morning of June 19, 2024, the National Electricity Market in Australia experienced a significant increase in market demand, reaching over 30,000 MW. This notable demand spike, unusual for a winter morning, is attributed to record cold temperatures across southeast Australia, including Melbourne experiencing its coldest morning in nearly two years. The high demand highlights the ongoing requirement for firming capacity, despite contributions from wind and large solar energy; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: Unusually cold weather conditions leading to increased electricity demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 19-06-2024. Its affected region is: Southeast Australia, particularly Victoria. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather-related demand surge. Its causes is: Record cold temperatures causing increased electricity demand.

title: Low IRPM eventuates (as low as 10.89%), as expected, on Tuesday evening 18th June 2024 .. reaching 120 minutes
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Low IRPM eventuates (as low as 10.89%), as expected, on Tuesday evening 18th June 2024 .. reaching 120 minutes" posted at 18-06-2024 07:24:00 PM is: The article discusses a prolonged period of low Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) conditions within the National Electricity Market (NEM) on June 18, 2024. The IRPM hit a low of 10.89% during peak demand at 6:25 PM, with a market demand peaking at 31,842MW. Contributing factors include the near-zero yield from Large Solar, reduced wind generation, and multiple coal unit outages (both planned and unplanned). These conditions led to heightened alerts as demand exceeded forecasts. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/System Instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: Low IRPM due to high demand and reduced supply. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-06-2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Supply-demand mismatch. Its causes are: High market demand, low solar yield, reduced wind generation, and multiple coal unit outages.

title: ‘Collector to Yass 3L’ network outage truncated, 3 weeks early
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "‘Collector to Yass 3L’ network outage truncated, 3 weeks early" posted at 18-06-2024 07:01:00 PM is: The scheduled outage of the Collector Wind Farm to Yass 3L 330kV line in the New South Wales region, initially planned from 3rd April to 8th July 2024, was concluded three weeks early, with service resuming on 18th June 2024. This early resolution of the outage helps alleviate the tight supply-demand balance in the region, especially given the low Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) recently observed and forecasted. The revocation of the 'N-CTYS_3L_WG_CLOSE' constraint set due to this early return is significant for the electricity market in southern NSW, connecting to Sydney and potentially easing conditions amid numerous 'forecast LOR2' (Lack of Reserve Level 2) alerts. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to short-term impact, starting from 18th June 2024. Its root cause is: Planned maintenance completed earlier than scheduled. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-06-2024 (same as the main date). Its affected region is: New South Wales, specifically the region connected by the Collector Wind Farm to Yass 3L line. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational (maintenance and grid operation). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Early completion of scheduled maintenance on the 330kV line.

title: Potentially challenging lack of reserve conditions in NSW over 18 to 21 June 2024
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Potentially challenging lack of reserve conditions in NSW over 18 to 21 June 2024" posted at 18-06-2024 04:30:00 PM is: The article discusses the predicted lack of reserve (LOR) conditions in New South Wales (NSW) over the period from 18th to 21st June 2024. LOR level 1 and 2 conditions are forecasted for several evenings and mornings, raising concerns about a tight energy supply in NSW. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) is urging a market response to mitigate these conditions. Offshore wind energy, though currently contentious due to political opposition, is highlighted as a potential solution to alleviate such low reserve conditions. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 18th to 21st June 2024. Its root_cause is: Predicted lack of reserve due to supply-demand dynamics. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18th to 21st June 2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Forecasted system stress. Its causes are: Tight energy supply outlook, suboptimal onshore wind production, political opposition to offshore wind solutions.

title: Mt Piper unit 1 offline till Monday 24th June with boiler tube leak
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Mt Piper unit 1 offline till Monday 24th June with boiler tube leak" posted at 18-06-2024 12:15:00 PM is: The Mt Piper Unit 1 went offline due to a boiler tube leak, impacting the NEM-wide IRPM forecasted to be lower than the previous evening. The generator's plans to take the unit offline overnight were communicated via a rebid received by AEMO at 21:12 on the previous day. Updates indicate that the unit's availability is expected to remain at 0MW until Sunday, 23rd June before returning to 720MW by Monday, 24th June 2024;. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: turbine/generator failures; Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (6 days); Its root_cause is: Boiler tube leak; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates are: 18-06-2024 to 24-06-2024; Its affected region is: Unknown; Its affected key users are: Unknown; Its cause type is: Mechanical failure; Its causes are: Boiler tube leak leading to unit outage.

title: NEM-wide IRPM might be below 15% during evenings the next 3 days… Tue 18th, Wed 19th and Thu 20th June 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM-wide IRPM might be below 15% during evenings the next 3 days… Tue 18th, Wed 19th and Thu 20th June 2024" posted at 18-06-2024 11:19:00 AM is: The article discusses the forecasted drop in the Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) below 15% for the evenings of June 18th, 19th, and 20th, 2024, across the National Electricity Market (NEM). On June 17th, the market demand surpassed 30,000MW, prompting concerns about grid stability for the following days as market demand might reach up to 32,000MW. AEMO's forecasts suggest these lower IRPM percentages are intended to stimulate a market response to the potential shortfall in reserves. Tuesday the 18th is expected to record an IRPM as low as 8.86%, with both Wednesday and Thursday meeting similar low thresholds. Such reductions this week could lead to grid instability and encourage responsive measures from the electricity market.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, affecting 18th to 20th June 2024. Its root_cause is: High market demand combined with low reserve margins predicted for evenings. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18th June 2024, 19th June 2024, 20th June 2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Predicted forecast event. Its causes are: Forecasted drop in reserve margins below 15%; Increased electricity demand exceeding 30,000MW.

title: 90-minute run of IRPM < 15% on Monday evening 17th June 2024
author: Paul McArdle

This condition led to increased market demand across various regions, particularly driven by cold weather. Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued notices indicating a forecasted tight supply-demand balance in the coming days. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its root_cause is: Cold weather and low wind power generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 17-06-2024. Its affected region is: NEM (National Electricity Market) regions in Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in affected regions. Its cause type is: Weather-related and generation insufficiency. Its causes are: Cold temperatures, low wind generation, increased electricity demand.

title: Cold temperatures and low wind push NEM-wide IRPM down to 11.76% on Monday 17th June 2024
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Cold temperatures and low wind push NEM-wide IRPM down to 11.76% on Monday 17th June 2024" posted at 17-06-2024 06:27:00 PM is: The National Electricity Market (NEM) experienced tight supply-demand conditions on June 17th, 2024, leading to a low Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) of 11.76% during the 17:55 dispatch interval. Contributing to this were cold winter temperatures across major cities and low wind output in South Australia, Victoria, and Tasmania. Additionally, outages at Eraring Unit 4, Tarong North, and Calide C4 further constrained available energy, resulting in prices exceeding $300 in all five NEM regions during the critical interval. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: Supply-demand imbalance caused by weather and outages. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 17-06-2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) regions including Brisbane, Sydney, Melbourne, Adelaide, and Hobart. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in NEM regions. Its cause type is: Multiple factors including weather and technical outages. Its causes are: Cold temperatures, low wind generation, and unit outages at Eraring Unit 4, Tarong North, and Calide C4.

title: When will we start seeing BDUs in dispatch?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "When will we start seeing BDUs in dispatch?" posted at 15-06-2024 04:35:00 PM is: The article discusses the implementation of the IESS Rule Change and the anticipated emergence of bi-directional units (BDUs) in the Australian Electricity Market Operator's (AEMO) dispatch systems. Although the rule change went live on 3rd June 2024, the first registration of a BDU is not expected until the end of June 2024. Existing scheduled battery systems will begin transitioning to the new BDU model starting mid-July, with the process completing by March 2025. This transition affects 26 registered batteries, which will adopt the new DUID model through a staggered process. The timeline is subject to adjustments by AEMO. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Starting from mid-July 2024 through March 2025. Its root cause is: Implementation of the IESS Rule Change by AEMO. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 3 June 2024 (IESS Rule Change effective date), end of June 2024 (expected first BDU registration), mid-July 2024 (start of existing systems' transition), March 2025 (end of transition process). Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Market participants and other stakeholders of AEMO. Its cause type is: Regulatory change. Its causes are: IESS Rule Change facilitating BDUs and transition of existing battery systems to a new dispatch model.

title: Who’s who in the ESOO Part 2
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Who’s who in the ESOO Part 2" posted at 14-06-2024 03:56:00 PM is: The article provides insights into the progression of new generation and storage projects in the National Electricity Market (NEM) as per the May update of the 2023 Electricity Statement of Opportunities (ESOO). It highlights that approximately 4.6 GW of new capacity, primarily battery storage, has advanced and includes a breakdown of specific projects anticipated for completion. However, concerns are raised around project status reporting and the reliability of the data due to the self-reported nature and infrequent updates. The development timeframes for Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) are anticipated to be lengthy, and data inconsistencies make forward-looking projections uncertain.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term due to ongoing project developments and completions. Its root_cause is: Updates and developments in new generation and storage projects in the NEM. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 14-06-2024. Its affected region is: The National Electricity Market regions including QLD, NSW, SA, VIC. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Data reporting and project progression issues. Its causes are: Lack of a robust project database, reliance on self-reported data, slow development timelines, inconsistency and incomplete data.

title: Afternoon volatility in TAS on Friday 14th June 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Afternoon volatility in TAS on Friday 14th June 2024" posted at 14-06-2024 03:54:00 PM is: The article details a period of price volatility in Tasmania on the afternoon of June 14, 2024, with electricity spot prices exceeding $1,000/MWh and peaking at $7,175.28/MWh. This volatility extends from a similar pattern detected on June 13, 2024. Contributing factors include the over-constrained dispatch due to a heavily utilized Basslink interconnector combined with reduced hydro and gas-fired power generation. Hydro Tasmania's storage levels and demand response from major energy users may have further influenced the market dynamics. Constraining factors such as wind farm operation also impacted the region. Essential constraints include the 'T::T_HA_GT_PM_4' constraint equation described by AEMO, reflecting on the Hadspen to George Town/Palmerston 220 kV line and further exacerbated by strong southward Basslink flows. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Over-constrained dispatch and reduced generation capacity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13-06-2024 to 14-06-2024. Its affected region is: Tasmania (TAS). Its affected key users are: Spot-exposed major energy users. Its cause type is: Market and operational constraints. Its causes are: Over-constrained dispatch due to Basslink flows, hydro generation downturn, constrained gas generation, modest wind farm output, and demand response.

title: An update on some coal-fired units, on Friday 14th June 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "An update on some coal-fired units, on Friday 14th June 2024" posted at 14-06-2024 01:30:00 PM is: The article discusses various updates on coal-fired units within the National Electricity Market (NEM). It highlights the extension of service for Eraring Power Station, steady expectations for Callide C4's return to service by 30th June 2024, and the unplanned outage of Tarong North, which is expected to last until 3rd July 2024. Additionally, Yallourn Unit 3 returned to service shortly after Tarong North's outage. These developments may affect market operations and potential electricity price fluctuations.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term, up to early July 2024. Its root cause is: Unplanned outages and service extensions at coal-fired units. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 14-06-2024, with additional relevant dates including 23-05-2024 (Eraring extension announcement), 10-06-2024 (Tarong North notified), 07-06-2024 (Yallourn Unit 3 outage), and expected return dates of 30-06-2024 (Callide C4) and 03-07-2024 (Tarong North). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (Australia). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational and maintenance-related issues. Its causes are: Extension of service for Eraring, unplanned outage at Tarong North, and Yallourn Unit 3's return from outage.

title: Market Notice 116985, QLD power system security intervention possible
author: Adam Kent
classified_content: The summary of the news "Market Notice 116985, QLD power system security intervention possible" posted at 13-06-2024 06:00:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) announced a possible intervention to maintain power system security in the Queensland region. AEMO identified that the synchronous generating units predicted for North QLD would be inadequate to maintain a secure operating state starting from 21:00 hours on 13/06/2024. In response, AEMO directed the Townsville power station Yabulu to synchronize and sustain the operation, achieving 83MW output between 21:05 and 22:00, with the direction lifted at 22:05. This type of market notice is common in other NEM regions but is the first for QLD.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short term (same day intervention). The root_cause is: Inadequate synchronization of generating units in North QLD to maintain power system security. The accident scale is: Unknown. The dates are: 13-06-2024. The affected region is: Queensland, specifically North QLD. The affected key users are: Electric power consumers and stakeholders in North QLD. The cause type is: Operational and technical. The causes are: Insufficient synchronization of generating units in North QLD, resulting in potential power system security failure.

title: Gas-fired generation running more strongly in TAS (and Tamar Valley CCGT for the first time) than it has over the past 5 years
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Gas-fired generation running more strongly in TAS (and Tamar Valley CCGT for the first time) than it has over the past 5 years" posted at 13-06-2024 09:58:00 AM is: The article highlights a significant increase in gas-fired generation in Tasmania, reaching its strongest level since May 2019. This rise is particularly noteworthy with the first operational use in over five years of the Tamar Valley CCGT, coinciding with persistent low wind power output and rising electricity prices in VIC, SA, and TAS. The situation raises discussions around the future role of gas-fired plants in energy transition, emphasizing their importance for peaking but pointing out challenges regarding emissions, economic viability, and fuel sourcing. The relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term. Its root_cause is: Increased demand for gas-fired generation due to low wind power output. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13-06-2024. Its affected region is: Tasmania. Its affected key users are: Energy producers and consumers in Tasmania. Its cause type is: Operational/Market-driven. Its causes are: Low wind yield, increased reliance on gas-fired generation, activation of Tamar Valley CCGT unit.

title: Wind yield drops again, prices rise in VIC, SA and TAS … and gas-fired generation is running strong
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Wind yield drops again, prices rise in VIC, SA and TAS … and gas-fired generation is running strong" posted at 13-06-2024 08:49:00 AM is: The article highlights a decrease in wind yield across the National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly affecting Tasmania, Victoria, and South Australia, with wind generation dropping below 1,000MW on the morning of June 13. As a consequence, regions have seen increased reliance on gas-fired generation, notably in Tasmania where the Tamar Valley CCGT is operational for the first time in over five years. This has driven electricity prices in these gas-firing regions above the $300/MWh ASX cap trigger price. Concurrently, Tasmania faces elevated demand due to its status as a winter-peaking region, and there is an increased incidence of 'forecast LORx' Market Notices from AEMO. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to short-term, during the winter period. Its root cause is: A significant drop in wind yield. The accident scale is: Regional within NEM states noted (TAS, VIC, SA). The dates are: 10th June 2024 - 13th June 2024. The affected region is: Tasmania, Victoria, and South Australia. The affected key users are: General electricity consumers and businesses in the affected regions. The cause type is: Natural decrease in wind yield. The causes (a summarized list of cause events): Drop in wind yield causing reduced generation capacity and increased reliance on gas-fired power, resulting in price hikes.

author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick overview of market outcomes on Sunday 9th June 2024" posted at 10-06-2024 12:00:00 PM is: The article provides a brief and retrospective overview of the electricity market outcomes on Sunday, 9th June 2024, using data visualized through the 'Trends Engine' in ez2view. title: Reviewing 270 days of bidding at Tallawarra B … following news of delays
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Reviewing 270 days of bidding at Tallawarra B … following news of delays" posted at 10-06-2024 09:11:00 AM is: The article discusses the delays faced by the Tallawarra B gas station, highlighting its registration, key operational dates, and subsequent unavailability for bidding during periods of market volatility. EnergyAustralia's Tallawarra B power station has encountered operational delays since its first output was recorded in January 2024, missing critical periods of market volatility due to its unavailability. Despite a scheduled operational date of 22nd June 2024, the date has been postponed multiple times. Monitoring continues to determine when the station will become active. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: gas pipeline failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: ongoing. Its root_cause is: operational delays in commissioning and making the gas station operational. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10-06-2024 to current, with key dates including the first output on 24th January 2024 and last non-zero availability on 16th April 2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: electricity market operators and consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: operational and commissioning delays. Its causes are: repeated postponements of the operational commencement date for the Tallawarra B gas station.

title: Case Study of NEM-wide Aggregate Scheduled Target on Thursday 18th January 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study of NEM-wide Aggregate Scheduled Target on Thursday 18th January 2024" posted at 08-06-2024 04:43:00 PM is: This article reviews the events of January 18, 2024, in the National Electricity Market (NEM), focusing on the Aggregate Scheduled Target during various dispatch intervals. A significant ramp-up of 2,068 MW occurred between 15:15 and 15:20, coinciding with a drop in spot prices in SA, VIC, and NSW, particularly in NSW, down to -$814.82/MWh. This event suggests economic curtailment of Semi-Scheduled VREs and raises questions about factors like rebidding or network congestion affecting this price fluctuation.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate – during the dispatch intervals. Its root_cause is: Rapid changes in scheduled and semi-scheduled capacity and price drops. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-01-2024. Its affected region is: SA, VIC, and NSW. Its affected key users are: Energy producers and consumers facing price variability. Its cause type is: Economic curtailment and market dynamics. Its causes are: A significant ramp-up in scheduled capacity, drastic price drops, potential rebidding influences, and network congestion.

title: Case Study of NEM-wide Aggregate Scheduled Target in early February 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study of NEM-wide Aggregate Scheduled Target in early February 2024" posted at 08-06-2024 03:39:00 PM is: The article highlights a trend observed in the Aggregate Scheduled Target (AggSchedTarget) across the National Electricity Market (NEM) during the first week of February 2024. Notably, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) had briefly forecasted an LOR3 (load shedding) event in Queensland on February 1, 2024, which was part of the broader assessment for the week. The focus is placed on different days, including a high Market Demand noted on February 4, 2024, and high demand expectations in New South Wales due to hot weather on February 5, 2024.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Early February 2024. Root_cause is: High market demand and weather conditions. Accident scale is: Unknown. Dates are: 01-02-2024 to 07-02-2024. Affected region is: NEM-wide, specifically Queensland and New South Wales. Affected key users are: Unknown. Cause type is: Market demand and weather-induced stress. Causes are: Brief LOR3 forecast, high market demand, hot weather conditions.

title: Eraring and Yallourn units offline on unplanned outages again
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Eraring and Yallourn units offline on unplanned outages again" posted at 08-06-2024 03:20:00 PM is: The article reports unplanned outages for three units at Eraring and Yallourn power stations, highlighting issues such as an Economiser Tube Leak for Eraring Unit 1 and Yallourn Unit 3, while Eraring Unit 2 is preparing for an upcoming unplanned maintenance outage. The units faced operational limitations, leading to the offline status, with decisions for outages made on June 5th and 7th, 2024. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term impact due to loss of generation capacity. Its root_cause is: Equipment failure, specifically Economiser Tube Leaks, and operational limitations. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: Outages initiated on 6th June 2024 for Eraring Unit 1, 7th June 2024 for Yallourn Unit 3, and Eraring Unit 2's outage beginning the following week. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and the grid operation in the affected regions, primarily in New South Wales. Its cause type is: Mechanical failures and maintenance issues. Its causes are: Economiser Tube Leaks and operational limitations leading to unplanned maintenance requirements.

title: Welcome to the Power Station of the Future – But Take Care
author: Ellise Janetzki
classified_content: The summary of the news "Welcome to the Power Station of the Future – But Take Care" posted at 07-06-2024 05:42:00 AM is: The article discusses the implementation of the 2024 IESS Rule Change in the Australian electricity market, specifically focusing on the advent of Integrated Resource Providers (IRPs) which integrate solar farms and battery energy storage systems. This transition marks a significant step towards genuinely dispatchable renewable energy, but it presents new challenges, particularly in accurately forecasting solar power production and managing the integration of solar and battery systems. The article highlights the potential issues arising from inaccuracies in solar forecasting, which can impact financial returns and power system security, suggesting that improved forecasting and management strategies are essential to mitigate these risks; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term as the changes are being implemented. Its root_cause is: Implementation of the 2024 IESS Rule Change, introducing Integrated Resource Providers. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 07-06-2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) regions in Australia. Its affected key users are: Solar farm operators, battery energy storage system operators, and energy market participants. Its cause type is: Regulatory change. Its causes are: Introduction of IRPs, challenges in solar forecasting accuracy, integration of solar and battery systems, market and operational complexities associated with the new system.

title: A spurt of price volatility in South Australia on Thursday 6th June 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A spurt of price volatility in South Australia on Thursday 6th June 2024" posted at 06-06-2024 04:24:00 PM is: A significant price volatility was observed in South Australia on Thursday, 6th June 2024, with spot prices spiking up to $10,000/MWh during a particular dispatch interval in the afternoon. This volatility was driven by limited import capacity from Victoria, coupled with modest market demand and a high reliance on gas-fired generators, as wind generation was relatively low and solar output was decreasing from its peak earlier in the day. Elevated prices were noted throughout the day. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Limited import capacity from Victoria and reliance on gas-fired generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 06-06-2024. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and market participants in South Australia. Its cause type is: Supply and demand imbalance. Its causes are: Limited import capacity from Victoria, modest wind generation, and decreasing solar generation.

title: Elevated prices in SA on 4th June 2024
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Elevated prices in SA on 4th June 2024" posted at 04-06-2024 08:16:00 PM is: The news reports a brief surge in energy prices in South Australia on June 4, 2024, occurring during the evening. Although projections indicated potential highs above $15,000 per MWh, actual prices exceeded $1,000 per MWh in only three dispatch intervals, with a peak of $3,936.60 per MWh between 18:20 and 18:30. The price spike was primarily attributed to a constrained Heywood interconnector, which restricted exports from Victoria, and light wind conditions, resulting in minimal wind power generation.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Brief (occurring on June 4, 2024, during the evening hours). Its root_cause is: Constrained interconnector and low wind generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 04-06-2024. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy market participants and potentially electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Operational constraints and weather-related. Its causes are: Constrained interconnector, limited export capacity from Victoria via the Heywood interconnector, and light wind conditions leading to reduced wind power generation.

title: Snapshot of Monday evening 3rd June 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Snapshot of Monday evening 3rd June 2024" posted at 03-06-2024 06:40:00 PM is: The article provides a quick snapshot of the National Electricity Market (NEM) during the evening of June 3rd, 2024, highlighting the market demand surpassing 31,000 MW, the highest since February 2024. Factors contributing to this include increased winter heating demand and reduced solar and wind yields in Victoria and across the NEM. Additionally, a brief forecast of a Lack of Reserve (LOR1) condition in South Australia was mentioned but quickly resolved; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate. Its root cause is: Increased winter heating demand and low renewable energy yields. Its accident scale is: N/A. Its dates are: 03-06-2024. Its affected region is: Victoria and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers across NEM. Its cause type is: Demand-supply imbalance. Its causes are: Elevated market demand due to heating needs and low energy yields from solar and wind sources.

title: Who’s who in the ESOO
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Who’s who in the ESOO" posted at 03-06-2024 12:57:00 PM is: The recent update to the Electricity Statement of Opportunities (ESOO) by the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has become outdated within 48 hours due to the announced extension of the Eraring Power Station's operation. The update highlighted project delays and shifts in the status of the National Electricity Market's (NEM) new capacity projects, with notable delays impacting storage projects such as big batteries and pumped hydro. The report indicates that the anticipated projects are now part of the reliability forecast, which signifies a shift in approach. Furthermore, the news discusses the departure of certain generation units from the market and emphasizes that, despite advances, the pace of projects moving to completion is not keeping up with coal retirements, raising concerns about the efficiency of current developments.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions . Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short to medium term, as projects are either delayed or advanced within the planning horizon. Its root_cause is: Project delays and operational changes in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-06-2024. Its affected region is: South Australia (SA), New South Wales (NSW), Victoria (VIC), Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational and planning changes. Its causes are: Delays in project completion, status changes in anticipated and committed projects, shifts in market operations such as extensions of existing power stations and withdrawal of certain generation units.

title: (The main part of) IESS goes live today – Monday 3rd June 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "IESS goes live today – Monday 3rd June 2024" posted at 03-06-2024 07:58:00 AM is: The Integrating Energy Storage Systems (IESS) reform has officially gone live within the National Electricity Market (NEM), introducing several significant changes, such as optional participation for small generation aggregators in frequency control ancillary services and updates to the registration and bidding for Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS). These changes affect financially responsible Market Participants and include material adjustments to non-energy cost recovery frameworks; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium to long-term, as the rule changes will reroute operational and financial aspects progressively. Its root_cause is: Implementation of IESS rule changes within NEM. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-06-2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Financially responsible Market Participants, Small Generation Aggregators, and entities involved with Battery Energy Storage Systems. Its cause type is: Policy reform. Its causes are: Reform to integrate energy storage systems into NEM, changes to ancillary services, registration adjustments, and cost recovery framework updates.

title: More detail on the poor wind yield through (most of!) April and May 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "More detail on the poor wind yield through (most of!) April and May 2024" posted at 01-06-2024 02:18:00 PM is: The article discusses the disappointing wind energy yield over April and May 2024 in comparison to previous years, highlighting lower production despite increased installed capacity. It points out that poor weather conditions were a key factor, and emphasizes the need for improved planning for firming capacity as coal units are phased out of the electricity grid. The article calls for strategic approaches to handle the variability and unreliability of wind energy production. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Notably impactful during the 61-day period of April and May 2024. Its root_cause is: Poor weather conditions impacting wind yield. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 01-06-2024. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural. Its causes are: Disappointing wind energy yield, increased installed capacity, poor weather conditions.

title: Highest-ever wind yield … *and* worst wind yield since June 2017!
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Highest-ever wind yield … *and* worst wind yield since June 2017!" posted at 01-06-2024 11:31:00 AM is: The article discusses the paradoxical scenario regarding wind energy production in the National Electricity Market (NEM) where both record high and notably low wind yields have been observed. It explains that while a gale led to peak wind yields of 8,431MW on 30th May 2024, the general trend for wind energy output in April and May 2024 was unusually low. This is compared to past statistics, particularly highlighting a significant low in June 2017. The capacity factors for April and May 2024 were 22.37% and 23.52%, respectively, indicating a boost due to the late-May gale, but still reflecting disappointing overall production levels. The data hints at changing wind patterns and the influence of geographic diversity and curtailment on wind energy output. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, with potential short-term analysis of wind yield impact. Its root_cause is: Variable wind patterns and geographical wind spread affecting yield. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: May 28, 2024, to June 1, 2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural factor in energy production. Its causes are: Variability in wind yield, peaks due to gale conditions, low output through April-May 2024, historical comparison with 2017.

title: How predictable is the wind (a short Case Study of Thursday evening 30th May 2024)?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "How predictable is the wind (a short Case Study of Thursday evening 30th May 2024)?" posted at 31-05-2024 10:11:00 AM is: The article discusses a significant wind event in the National Electricity Market (NEM) over late May 2024, where a high-pressure system gave way to increased wind patterns, culminating in a record wind power generation late on Thursday, 30th May 2024, before declining again by Friday, 31st May. The Australian Energy Market Operator's (AEMO) short-term forecasts had reasonably anticipated this increase, although the earlier forecast from 24th May had significantly underestimated the wind power capability by approximately 6,200MW. This highlights the challenges in predicting wind output and implications for system reliability and commercial considerations. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Late May 2024. Its root_cause is: Changes in atmospheric conditions leading to variability in wind patterns. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28th May 2024 to 31st May 2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy providers and consumers within the NEM. Its cause type is: Natural atmospheric changes. Its causes are: Transition from a high-pressure system to increasing wind patterns affecting wind power generation output.

title: New all-time record, for NEM-wide wind production on Thursday evening 30th May 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "New all-time record, for NEM-wide wind production on Thursday evening 30th May 2024" posted at 30-05-2024 08:48:00 PM is: This article reports an all-time record for NEM-wide wind production on the evening of May 30, 2024, reaching a peak of 8,431 MW during the 21:05 dispatch interval. After a largely disappointing early May, wind generation surged in the final days, culminating in this unprecedented output which exceeded the previous record set in July 2023. The wind production started to decline later in the evening. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate but short-term. Its root_cause is: Unusually high wind speeds leading to increased wind farm output. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-05-2024. Its affected region is: NEM (National Electricity Market, Australia). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural. Its causes are: Surge in wind energy generation due to favorable weather conditions.

title: Aggregate wind production NEMwide ramps up (close to all-time maximum!) to round out an otherwise low-yield May 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Aggregate wind production NEMwide ramps up (close to all-time maximum!) to round out an otherwise low-yield May 2024" posted at 30-05-2024 12:05:00 PM is: The news reports a significant surge in wind power generation across the National Electricity Market (NEM) nearing an all-time high, as it reaches 7,761MW, just shy of the record 8,132MW achieved in July 2023. This spike contrasts with earlier predictions and the typically lower wind yield expected in May 2024. Forecasts by AEMO suggest potential generation could hit 7,940MW by the end of the day, indicating a substantial increase from earlier conservative estimates of 2,200MW, reflecting the challenges in predicting Variable Renewable Energy (VRE) resources accurately. Despite this optimistic outlook, expected curtailment might limit actual power generation.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (immediate surge in wind production and potential pricing effects); Its root_cause is: Natural variation in wind resource availability, coupled with forecasting adjustments. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 30-05-2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market-wide. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural variation and forecasting adjustments. Its causes is: Variation in natural wind resources, AEMO's revised forecasts for energy output, potential curtailment of energy production.

title: What impact did the extension to Eraring have on traded prices for NSW Futures?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What impact did the extension to Eraring have on traded prices for NSW Futures?" posted at 30-05-2024 08:43:00 AM is: The article discusses the impact of the decision to extend the service life of Eraring Power Station on the futures market in New South Wales (NSW), highlighting a drop in traded prices for electricity futures contracts coinciding with speculation and rumors regarding the extension, followed by a rise in prices post-announcement. The market reacted to the uncertainty around Eraring's operations, with sentiment-driven trading observed. Factors like spot price volatility and the decision to extend Eraring’s operation through 2025 influenced contract price volatility, although the exact triggers remain unclear. The extension announcement led to increased futures prices for quarters beyond 2024 Q2, despite Eraring's availability being assumed for some quarters. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to mid-term impact due to futures contract adjustments. Its root_cause is: Market reaction to the extension of the Eraring Power Station's service life. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23rd May 2024 (announcement of extension). Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity traders and potentially large consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Policy and market sentiment response. Its causes are: Speculation about Eraring's closure, the decision to extend its operation, spot price volatility, and market sentiment changes.

title: (Easily* the) highest aggregate wind production, thus far in May 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Easily the highest aggregate wind production, thus far in May 2024" posted at 29-05-2024 07:53:00 AM is: On the morning of May 29, 2024, it was recorded that wind energy production has peaked briefly above 6,600MW, marking the highest aggregate wind yield for May 2024, despite the generally low wind yield experienced in April and May 2024. This increase was anticipated based on AEMO's forecasts. However, this peak is still substantially below the all-time maximum. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Eraring Unit 1 comes offline for Economiser Tube Leak" posted at 28-05-2024 09:40:00 AM is: Eraring Unit 1 at the Eraring Power Station has been taken offline due to an economiser tube leak. title: The ‘N::N_CTYS_2’ constraint equation will be invoked for another 11 days … out till Friday 7th June 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The ‘N::N_CTYS_2’ constraint equation will be invoked for another 11 days … out till Friday 7th June 2024" posted at 27-05-2024 04:03:00 PM is: The article discusses the extended invocation of the 'N::N_CTYS_2' constraint equation in the National Electricity Market (NEM), specifically impacting the Collector WF – Yass 3L 330KV Line in the NSW region. This constraint was initially set due to a scheduled outage from April 3 to May 27, 2024, and has now been extended until June 7, 2024. This extension influenced market outcomes, including dispatch results and inter-regional transfer limits. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued notices indicating a Forecast Lack of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) condition for June 3, 2024, indicating a tight supply-demand balance. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to short-term until 7th June 2024. Its root cause is: Scheduled outage extension of Collector WF – Yass 3L 330KV Line. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 27-05-2024 to 07-06-2024. Its affected region is: NSW. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in the NSW region. Its cause type is: Planned outage and constraint invocation. Its causes are: Extension of scheduled network outage, and constraint on wind farm outputs impacting supply-demand balance.

author: Paul McArdle

classified_content: The summary of the news "Low wind yield (again!) during sunset … this time Saturday evening 25th May 2024" posted at 25-05-2024 06:06:00 PM is: The article discusses the continuation of low wind yield during sunset for three consecutive days, including 25th May 2024, across the National Electricity Market (NEM). Despite healthy electricity prices, wind yield was low, attributed mainly to unfavourable wind conditions rather than network or economic curtailment. This condition caused concern over electricity supply-demand balance in the face of delays in renewable projects and coal power phase-out. Only Mt Emerald Wind Farm in Queensland showed better performance at 50% capacity. The article ties these conditions to broader challenges of maintaining electricity reliability as highlighted in recent updates from AEMO. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). title: Callide C updates – operations (<40 days till RTS!), and in the courts
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "Callide C updates – operations (<40 days till RTS!), and in the courts" posted at 24-05-2024 11:48:00 AM is: The news covers developments related to the Callide C Power Station, specifically focusing on the anticipated return to service for the Callide C4 unit, which is expected to be operational by June 30, 2024. Meanwhile, Callide C3 has successfully returned to full operational capacity as of April 1, 2024. However, the ongoing legal and insolvency proceedings involving Callide C4 continue to face challenges. Forensic engineer Sean Brady has yet to complete his investigation report on the Callide C4 explosion. Court matters include an independent investigation authorized by a Federal Court ruling (docket number QUD541/2023) and a case brought by the AER (QUD61/2024). Stakeholders such as CS Energy and Sev.en Global Investments have limited public updates. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium-term, with implications for power supply stability as Callide C4 approaches its return to service. Its root_cause is: A significant explosion leading to extended outages and legal investigations. Its accident scale is: Significant, impacting operations and sparking multiple legal actions. Its dates are: 25th May 2021 (initial explosion), leading to ongoing updates and legal proceedings through 2024. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Regional electricity consumers in Queensland who rely on the output from Callide C Power Station. Its cause type is: Operational and legal challenges due to the explosion. Its causes are: Explosion at Callide C4, ongoing legal and forensic investigations, delays in the sale process due to unresolved court matters.

author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM-wide aggregate wind yield under 500MW on Thursday evening 23rd May 2024" posted at 23-05-2024 05:23:00 PM is: The NEM (National Electricity Market) experienced an unusually low aggregate wind yield of under 500 MW on 23rd May 2024, coinciding with sunset, as monitored by NEMwatch. This incident reflects a broader trend of low wind yields observed in April and May. With an installed capacity of 11,347 MW, the instantaneous capacity factor was a mere 3.5%. Though solar yield was low, it was not zero. Factors contributing to low wind include reduced wind resources and potential curtailments for network or economic reasons. Updating long-range wind production statistics is suggested. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-05-2024. Its affected region is: NEM-wide, which covers several states in Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers across the NEM. Its cause type is: Resource deficiency and operational constraints. Its causes are: 1) Low underlying wind resource; 2) Possible curtailments for network or economic reasons.

title: Eraring Power Station closure delayed by 2 years (or maybe 4?)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Eraring Power Station closure delayed by 2 years (or maybe 4?)" posted at 23-05-2024 10:46:00 AM is: The NSW Government and Origin Energy have agreed to delay the closure of Eraring Power Station, initially planned for August 19, 2025, by at least two years, potentially extending it to 2029. This decision aims to maintain reliability and manage price risks in the electricity market. Origin Energy will continue operations and generate at least 6 TWh of electricity annually during FY2026 and FY2027, while also potentially receiving compensation for operating losses. author: Paul McArdle

It also touches upon the 'N::N_CTYS_2' constraint equation affecting the dispatch decisions for the Queanbeyan BESS by driving the CPD Price below the Market Price Floor.
