title: 2024 Consultation on Forecasting Accuracy Report Methodology
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "2024 Consultation on Forecasting Accuracy Report Methodology" posted at 30-01-2024 12:51:00 PM is: AEMO is consulting on the methodology for its Forecasting Accuracy Report, which evaluates the organization's long-term electricity demand and supply forecasts. These forecasts are critical for various reliability and planning operations, such as the Medium Term Projected Assessment of System Adequacy, Electricity Statement of Opportunities, and the Integrated System Plan. The University of Adelaide has been involved in reviewing the methodology, resulting in twenty-six recommendations that form the basis of the consultation. Key upcoming dates include closing of the first stage submissions on 5 February 2024, with the final determination expected by 20 May 2024. This consultation occurs every four years, with the prior one having been conducted in 2020; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Longer-term impact, potentially up to the next consultation in 2028. Its root_cause is: Regular review cycle for improving forecast accuracy and reliability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-01-2024 to 20-05-2024. Its affected region is: Potential impact across regions outlined in AEMO's forecasts. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants relying on demand-supply forecasts. Its cause type is: Consulting on methodology improvements and stakeholder engagement. Its causes are: Recommendations from the University of Adelaide, regular forecasting review requirements, ensuring accuracy and reliability in forecasts.

title: Federal Court rules about Callide C – independent investigation authorised
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "Federal Court rules about Callide C – independent investigation authorised" posted at 29-01-2024 08:14:00 PM is: The Federal Court has authorised a special investigation into the catastrophic explosions and failures at the Callide C power station in Queensland, following a series of incidents beginning with a major explosion on 25th May 2021 and the collapse of a cooling tower on 31st October 2022. This investigation, prompted by Sev.en Global Investments after IG Power Callide went into voluntary administration, will look into the causes of these incidents and any potential claims arising from them. The Callide C4 unit has been offline since the explosion, while Callide C3 was out following the cooling tower collapse, with expected return-to-service dates in mid-2024. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term, ongoing since May 2021 with expected implications until mid-2024. Its root_cause is: Unknown, pending investigation. Its accident scale is: Significant, affecting multiple units and causing prolonged outages. Its dates are: 25th May 2021 and 31st October 2022 (event dates); 29th January 2024 (investigation authorisation date). Its affected region is: Central Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown, but likely large-scale electricity consumers and stakeholders in the region. Its cause type is: Mechanical failure and structural collapse. Its causes are: Explosion at Unit C4 and cooling tower collapse affecting Unit C3.

title: Evening volatility in QLD and NSW on Monday 29th January 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Evening volatility in QLD and NSW on Monday 29th January 2024" posted at 29-01-2024 07:11:00 PM is: The report highlights evening electricity market volatility in Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW) that started at the 17:20 dispatch interval and persisted until at least 18:55. During this period, QLD's market demand peaked at 10,050MW, while NSW's market demand climbed to 11,411MW. Additionally, total NEM-wide market demand exceeded 30,000MW. The volatility was exacerbated by bound constraints on the QNI and Directlink interconnectors, restricting supply from NSW and reducing the Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) for the QLD economic island. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, during the dispatch interval and possibly extending into the evening. Its root_cause is: High market demand and interconnector constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 29-01-2024. Its affected region is: Queensland and New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in QLD and NSW. Its cause type is: Market demand and infrastructure constraints. Its causes are: High market demand in QLD and NSW, and bound constraints on QNI and Directlink interconnectors.

title: AEMO briefly forecasts LOR3 (i.e. load shedding) in QLD on Thu 1st Feb 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO briefly forecasts LOR3 (i.e. load shedding) in QLD on Thu 1st Feb 2024" posted at 29-01-2024 09:33:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) initially forecasted a Lack of Reserve Level 3 (LOR3), indicating potential load shedding, in Queensland (QLD) for February 1, 2024. This forecast suggested possible electricity interruptions due to insufficient reserves for a few periods during the day, with a maximum load interruption forecast of 38 MW at 0930 hrs and 62 MW at 1130 hrs. Shortly after, the forecast was downgraded to LOR2, reducing the likelihood of load shedding as the market notices indicated updated capacity reserve availability versus requirements. This forecast is part of AEMO's process to prompt market responses and mitigate supply issues. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, specifically February 1, 2024. Its root_cause is: Suspect reserve shortfalls leading to load-shedding alerts. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 01/02/2024. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Forecast. Its causes is: Potential reserve shortfall, prompting LOR3 and then LOR2 warnings for specific periods on February 1, 2024, with AEMO investigations underway to determine validity.

title: Evening volatility in QLD and NSW on Sunday 28th January 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Evening volatility in QLD and NSW on Sunday 28th January 2024" posted at 28-01-2024 06:03:00 PM is: The article provides a snapshot of electricity spot prices increasing in Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW) on January 28, 2024, using data from NEMwatch. Despite the weather in Brisbane improving from the previous day with rain and cloud cover, solar energy yield was subdued. This contributed to the volatility observed in the electricity market prices. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (likely lasting only through the day's weather conditions). Its root_cause is: Reduced solar yield due to weather conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-01-2024. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather conditions. Its causes are: Rain and cloud cover reducing solar energy yield.

title: QLD ‘Market Demand’ reaches 10,210MW on Sat 27th Jan 2024, and looks set to climb further
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "QLD ‘Market Demand’ reaches 10,210MW on Sat 27th Jan 2024, and looks set to climb further" posted at 27-01-2024 06:02:00 PM is: The electricity market demand in the Queensland (QLD) region surged to 10,210 MW on Saturday, January 27, 2024, nearing the 'Red Zone,' shortly after a new peak demand level was set on January 22. Despite the high demand, the spot price exceeded $1,000/MWh for the first dispatch interval, triggering alerts, and the Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) dropped to 9.5%, suggesting strained supply. Constrained northward QNI flow and a southward Directlink, along with solar generation decline at sunset, contributed to the situation, which also followed disruptions from Tropical Cyclone Kirrily. This incident has resulted in market tensions and uncertainty in predictions for near-term price and demand levels. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Near-term. Its root_cause is: High market demand and constrained flows affecting supply margins. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 27-01-2024. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational and natural disruption. Its causes are: High electricity demand, constraints in electricity flow, and effects of a tropical cyclone.

title: Forecast evening demand for QLD falling away for Thu 27th Jan 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Forecast evening demand for QLD falling away for Thu 27th Jan 2024" posted at 27-01-2024 04:11:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) provided an update on the electricity market in Queensland (QLD) for the evening of January 27th, 2024, indicating a decline in forecast market demand and pricing volatility. Furthermore, a previously issued forecast Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition for the evening was canceled, with only a less severe LOR1 forecast remaining in place. Attention is drawn to a new forecast LOR2 appearing for January 30th, 2024, suggesting potential future supply concerns. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate, primarily affecting January 27th, 2024, and potential implications for January 30th, 2024. Its root cause is: Adjustments in forecasted demand and supply conditions. Its accident scale is: N/A (no accident). Its dates are: 27-01-2024. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and market participants in QLD. Its cause type is: Operational and forecast adjustments. Its causes are: Decline in forecasted market demand, subsiding pricing volatility, cancellation of forecast LOR2, existence of LOR1 condition, and emergence of new forecast LOR2 for the future date.

title: AEMO updates – has contracted RERT and *may* dispatch, on Sat 27th Jan 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO updates – has contracted RERT and *may* dispatch, on Sat 27th Jan 2024" posted at 27-01-2024 01:07:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has updated its previous announcement by confirming that it has contracted a Reliability and Emergency Reserve Trader (RERT) for the Queensland region. This reserve may be dispatched to ensure the reliability of the power system during the specified period from 17:30 to 21:00 hours on January 27, 2024. While the exact need to activate these reserves is yet to be determined, AEMO will issue further advisement if activation is necessary. Current forecasts suggest slightly lower demand than initially anticipated, along with some wavering in LOR (Lack of Reserve) conditions and ongoing forecasts of price volatility; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: January 27, 2024, 17:30 to 21:00 hours. Its root_cause is: Anticipated grid instability due to high demand forecasts and potential reserve shortfall. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 27-01-2024. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Predicted operational issue. Its causes is: High electricity demand forecast, potential reserve shortfall, need for grid reliability intervention.

title: A handy reminder from the BOM about why QLD is feeling so hot – on Saturday 27th January 2024
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2024-01-22 QLD (massively higher!) all time max demand
classified_content: The summary of the news "A handy reminder from the BOM about why QLD is feeling so hot – on Saturday 27th January 2024" posted at 27-01-2024 12:50:00 PM is: The Bureau of Meteorology highlighted why Queensland is experiencing extreme heat, emphasizing the high dew point as a significant factor. This has contributed to a massively higher all-time maximum demand for electricity recorded in the region.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, impacting the current demand levels. Its root_cause is: Extreme heat conditions due to high dew points. Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates is: 27-01-2024; Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD); Its affected key users is: Unknown; Its cause type is: Environmental; Its causes is: High dew point, leading to increased temperatures and electricity demand.

title: AEMO announces intention to commence RERT negotiations in QLD–on Saturday 27th January 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO announces intention to commence RERT negotiations in QLD–on Saturday 27th January 2024" posted at 27-01-2024 12:10:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) plans to enter into RERT (Reliability and Emergency Reserve Trader) negotiations in Queensland due to anticipated high evening electricity demand on 27th January 2024. The negotiations aim to secure additional reserves between 17:30 and 21:00 hours, although activation may not occur throughout this entire period. Recent power unit outages could have influenced this decision, reflecting system supply dynamics previously observed on 22nd January 2024. Its relevance level is: Level 1; Its category is: grid/system instability; Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term; Its root_cause is: anticipated high electricity demand combined with recent unit outages; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates is: 27-01-2024; Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia; Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in Queensland; Its cause type is: Market intervention due to supply-demand balance concerns; Its causes is: High forecasted electricity demand, recent outages of coal units MPP_1 and TARONG#2.

title: AEMO forecasts impressive evening demand in QLD on Saturday 27th Jan 2024 (middle of a 3-day weekend)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO forecasts impressive evening demand in QLD on Saturday 27th Jan 2024 (middle of a 3-day weekend)" posted at 27-01-2024 11:47:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) predicts a significant market demand in Queensland for the evening of January 27, 2024, reaching up to 10,350MW. This expected demand is part of a trend following an all-time high demand of 11,036MW recorded on January 22, 2024. Despite increased demand expected during this period, which includes a 3-day weekend and high temperatures, AEMO has issued a forecast for a Low Reserve Condition (LOR2) due to anticipated constraints, such as reduced power flow with New South Wales and the coinciding sunset. This situation is contributing to ongoing price volatility in the market. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, specifically 27th January 2024. Its root_cause is: High electricity demand and potential grid constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 27-01-2024. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Forecasted demand and grid reserve conditions. Its causes is: High market demand, LOR2 forecast due to potential grid instability with constraints like flows with NSW and sunset timing.

title: Evening burst of volatility in (QLD and) NSW on Friday 26th January 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Evening burst of volatility in (QLD and) NSW on Friday 26th January 2024" posted at 26-01-2024 07:03:00 PM is: The article reports a brief period of volatility in the electricity markets of New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD), Australia, on the evening of January 26, 2024. During this period, electricity prices in QLD spiked to $13,990.01/MWh during the 18:45 dispatch interval. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD), Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market volatility. title: AEMO’s forecasts for higher ‘Market Demand’ in NSW on Thursday evening 25th January 2024 don’t materialise
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO’s forecasts for higher ‘Market Demand’ in NSW on Thursday evening 25th January 2024 don’t materialise" posted at 26-01-2024 04:39:00 PM is: The article discusses the unexpected drop in market demand for electricity in New South Wales (NSW) on the evening of January 25, 2024. AEMO had forecasted a higher demand due to a predicted deep Lack of Reserve 2 (LOR2) condition, but an unforeseen cooler weather change caused demand to be lower than anticipated. The initial forecasts made by ST PASA and P30 predispatch failed to account for this weather shift, highlighting the challenges in accurately predicting electricity market demand. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, on and around January 25, 2024. Its root_cause is: A sudden change in weather conditions leading to lower electricity demand. Its accident scale is: Not applicable. Its dates are: 25-01-2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and suppliers in NSW. Its cause type is: Environmental/weather-related. Its causes are: A cooler weather change leading to lower electricity demand than forecasted.

title: First output at Tallawarra B
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "First output at Tallawarra B" posted at 24-01-2024 01:50:00 PM is: The article discusses the initial output from the Tallawarra B gas peaking power station during its synchronisation testing phase. Owned by EnergyAustralia, the facility is undergoing various tests and will eventually begin commercial operations in late February. Details from EnergyAustralia's updates on January 25th, 2024, confirmed the timeline for its operational readiness. While EnergyAustralia has not publicly shared extensive details on their website or social media as of yet, it is expected that further announcements will follow as the situation develops. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term testing phase with no significant market impact until commercial operations begin in late February. title: Unpicking the generation mix in QLD over the past four days
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Unpicking the generation mix in QLD over the past four days" posted at 22-01-2024 01:29:00 PM is: Over the past four days, extreme humidity and high temperatures in Queensland have caused record electricity demand, with expectations of demand records being broken again soon. The market has seen very high late afternoon and early evening prices, particularly on Thursday, Friday, and Sunday, with price caps reached on some days. The electricity supply has been tight, with low wind generation and contributions from hydro, liquid fuel, and battery storage. Key generation components include black coal, natural gas, coal seam methane, and solar, with varying performance noted. Outages and deratings have impacted black coal capacity, while hydro generation has been affected by outages due to Cyclone Jasper. Wind generation availability has been suboptimal, negatively impacting supply dynamics. As the situation remains in flux, further insights into the supply side and potential future developments are critical. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate and ongoing as of January 2024. Its root cause is: Extreme weather conditions including high temperatures and humidity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-01-2024 to 22-01-2024. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Queensland. Its cause type is: Weather-induced demand surge. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Extreme temperatures, humidity, record electricity demand, generation supply tightness, outages and limited availability of wind and hydro generation.

classified_content: The summary of the news "‘Market Demand’ briefly peaks at a new all-time maximum in QLD (at 17:30 on Friday 19th January 2024)" posted at 19-01-2024 07:09:00 PM is: On January 19th, 2024, Queensland experienced an unprecedented peak in market demand for electricity, reaching 10,161 MW at 17:30 NEM time, exceeding the previous all-time high by 42 MW. The demand far surpassed forecasts due to hotter-than-expected conditions. This surge in demand strained supply in the region, despite increased contributions from "Keeping the Lights On Services" and modest output from wind farms. Prices soared to $14,928.01/MWh, approaching the market price cap. Constraints on interconnectors led to a Queensland-only "Economic Island" situation with a tight reserve margin of 8% IRPM. Actual LOR1 (Lack of Reserve) conditions were noted during this period, and heightened volatility was observed.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate and potential effects until January 22nd, 2024, when demand is expected to rise further. Its root cause is: Higher than forecasted demand due to unexpected heat. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 19-01-2024. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and market participants in Queensland. Its cause type is: Demand surge due to unexpected weather conditions. Its causes are: Unanticipated high temperatures leading to increased electricity demand, limited contributions from solar and wind, and interconnector constraints creating a localized supply-demand imbalance.

author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Clean Energy Finance analysis says Eraring does not need to be extended" posted at 19-01-2024 01:03:00 PM is: A report by Clean Energy Finance, led by Tim Buckley, suggests that New South Wales can avoid electricity shortages without the need to extend the operation of the Eraring coal-fired power station beyond its planned closure in 2025. title: Power outage in Western Australia (incl Kalgoorlie) from Wed 17th Jan 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Power outage in Western Australia (incl Kalgoorlie) from Wed 17th Jan 2024" posted at 19-01-2024 10:53:00 AM is: Extreme weather has caused significant power outages in various parts of Western Australia, particularly impacting Kalgoorlie, Coolgardie, and the Goldfields region. A storm that began on Wednesday, 17th January, led to tens of thousands of customers losing power, with impacts on critical services like fuel, water supply, and internet connections. Western Power is working to restore electricity to approximately 15,000 homes and businesses, with five towers down and repairs expected to take an extended time. Restoration times remain uncertain, and outages may continue for up to seven days. Power restoration began on Friday, but around 1,600 residents were still affected. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Forecasts indicate that demand may reach an unprecedented level, potentially hitting 10,752 MW, which is an all-time maximum. AEMO is seeking a market response to address this predicted shortfall, signaling the challenges posed due to volatile weather impacting demand predictions. title: Why generation offers diverge from variable cost
author: Greg Williams
classified_content: The summary of the news "Why generation offers diverge from variable cost" posted at 17-01-2024 11:22:00 AM is: The article explores the reasons behind the divergence of generation offers from variable costs in the National Electricity Market (NEM). It explains how opportunity costs play a crucial role in the bidding strategies of various electricity generation resources, including fuel-limited ones like hydro and thermal power stations, due to factors like fuel scarcity and operational inflexibilities. These pricing strategies are described as socially efficient during times of scarcity or overabundance, but require vigilant monitoring to prevent market manipulation. The piece also discusses the unique trading characteristics of batteries and pumped hydro and how these influence electricity prices over different time horizons. It concludes with implications for policy in the context of the NEM’s transition to a low-emission generation mix.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Market dynamics driven by opportunity costs and resource inflexibilities. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 17-01-2024 11:22:00 AM. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market strategy and operational constraints. Its causes are: Opportunity cost considerations, fuel scarcity, operational inflexibility, price-sensitive consumption, trading behavior of special resources like batteries and pumped hydro.

classified_content: The summary of the news "Forecast temperature rising in Queensland for Thu 18th Jan 2024 (and Fri 19th and Mon 22nd)" posted at 16-01-2024 12:01:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued a local temperature alert for Dalby, southern Queensland, forecasting temperatures of 37°C or higher from 18th to 20th January 2024. Such temperatures can impact various generation plants, including peaking units, combined cycle units, coal units, solar plants, wind farms, and batteries. These high-temperature alerts follow recent tight supply-demand balance warnings (LOR2 and LOR1) for Queensland, although these were canceled on 16th January 2024 following a revised forecast. AEMO advises market participants to adjust their available capacity submissions as needed. title: CleanCo’s CEO Tom Metcalfe provides an update on outage at Barron Gorge Hydro Power Station
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "CleanCo’s CEO Tom Metcalfe provides an update on outage at Barron Gorge Hydro Power Station" posted at 15-01-2024 03:38:00 PM is: CleanCo's Barron Gorge Hydro Power Station, which has a capacity of 66MW, suffered significant damage due to flooding from Cyclone Jasper, with the highest water levels recorded in its 60-year history. The earthen embankment was eroded by the floodwaters, requiring reconstruction for operational control. While the concrete spillway remained intact, repairs are necessary for a return to full service, expected by the end of June 2024. The station may operate at reduced capacity initially as a temporary solution, with full operation anticipated to resume by July 1st, 2024. The damage assessment is ongoing, and repair costs are yet to be estimated. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. title: The effect of opportunity cost on wholesale prices in the NEM
author: Greg Williams
classified_content: The summary of the news "The effect of opportunity cost on wholesale prices in the NEM" posted at 15-01-2024 02:59:00 PM is: The article explains how the economic concept of opportunity cost significantly affects wholesale electricity prices in the National Electricity Market (NEM). It highlights that despite the simplification of electricity markets where prices often mirror the variable costs of the marginal generator, actual market dynamics are influenced by factors such as operational constraints and opportunity cost considerations. These factors lead generators to offer prices beyond variable costs, driven by the need for generating units to be dispatched or kept in reserve. The article also illustrates how increasing renewable generation impacts price zones, with opportunity costs often resulting in short-run marginal costs below variable costs. This is the first of a series highlighting the importance of opportunity cost in wholesale price formation.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, as it reflects the continuous influence of opportunity cost on market dynamics. Its root_cause is: Consideration of opportunity cost in electricity pricing and market operations. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 15-01-2024. Its affected region is: The National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Generators and market participants in the NEM. Its cause type is: Economic and market dynamics. Its causes are: Opportunity cost considerations, operational constraints, renewable generation impacts.

title: In November 2023, FERC released Final Report on lessons from Winter Storm Elliott (the Christmas 2022 storm)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "In November 2023, FERC released Final Report on lessons from Winter Storm Elliott (the Christmas 2022 storm)" posted at 15-01-2024 11:39:00 AM is: The FERC's final report on Winter Storm Elliott, which caused significant power outages in the U.S. during December 2022, identified that freezing, fuel issues, and mechanical/electrical failures were primary causes of generating unit failures. Recommendations include improving cold weather performance and reliable standards for both electric generators and natural gas systems due to their interdependence, highlighting a need for urgency before the current winter season. title: The early wind farms reaching 20 years: how are they performing?
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "The early wind farms reaching 20 years: how are they performing?" posted at 11-01-2024 09:38:00 AM is: The article delves into the performance and lifespan of some of the earliest utility-scale wind farms in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM) as they approach or surpass their 20-year operational mark. The Challicum Hills Wind Farm, among others, is highlighted for its pioneering role. Despite facing challenges such as declining capacity factors, changing operational status, and external market pressures like negative pricing and curtailment, there is optimism about extending their operational life beyond initial expectations. However, revenue evaluations suggest that while these wind farms have been hedged under long-term PPAs and have earned sustainable revenues, there are critical factors impacting their commercial viability, requiring deeper financial analyses. Its relevance level is: Level 3; Its category is: Energy policy; Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term; Its root_cause is: Aging wind farms and market conditions; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates are: 11-01-2024; Its affected region is: Australia; Its affected key users are: Unknown; Its cause type is: Operational and market conditions; Its causes are: Aging infrastructure, changing operational designations, historical PPA agreements, and economic factors like negative pricing.

title: Inverter fire at EPIC Energy’s Mannum 2 Solar Farm
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Inverter fire at EPIC Energy’s Mannum 2 Solar Farm" posted at 10-01-2024 04:29:00 PM is: A fire incident occurred at the Mannum 2 Solar Farm in South Australia, involving an inverter. The solar farm, owned by Epic Energy, is in the commissioning phase and had begun sending power to the grid despite the RenewEconomy article stating otherwise. The fire was reported around 09:15 NEM time, leading to a reduction in the Power Availability Status and Market Volume bids to 0 MW initially, but curiously ramping back up to 5 MW soon after. No explicit mention of the fire was made in the rebid reasons submitted to AEMO. There is an ongoing investigation into the fire with more questions than answers as of now.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Inverter fire during commissioning at solar farm. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10-01-2024; and 09-01-2024 for the occurrence. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational failure. Its causes are: Inverter malfunction leading to fire incident during solar farm commissioning.

title: What’s been happening at Stanwell unit 1 since September 2023? (Part 1)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What’s been happening at Stanwell unit 1 since September 2023? (Part 1)" posted at 10-01-2024 03:11:00 PM is: The article discusses the operational history and current challenges facing Stanwell Unit 1, a coal-fired generator in the National Electricity Market (NEM), highlighting the unit's transition through three phases over recent years, including a recent operational period marked by fixed load bidding due to stress in high-pressure bypass valve pipework. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers in the National Electricity Market. Its cause type is: Technical malfunction. Its causes are: High-pressure bypass valve pipework stress and fatigue in Stanwell Unit 1 leading to fixed load operations.

title: Hiccups at Loy Yang A4 (Part 1)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Hiccups at Loy Yang A4 (Part 1)" posted at 09-01-2024 12:06:00 PM is: The Loy Yang A unit 4 experienced multiple trips over several days due to ongoing 'safety valve issues,' after initially returning online from a forced outage that lasted from 29th December 2023 to 6th January 2024. This series of trips highlights the challenges faced by complex coal generation units, particularly when coming back from outages. Historical data shows a good availability performance for this unit over the previous year, indicating an unusual deviation in recent weeks. The news also briefly notes the unrelated progress on the Callide C3 cooling tower repair. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, affecting supply stability and potential prices. Its root_cause is: Safety valve issues leading to multiple unexpected trips. Its accident scale is: Localized to Loy Yang A unit 4. Its dates are: 29th December 2023 to 9th January 2024. Its affected region is: Loy Yang A, presumably impacting Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in the region relying on Loy Yang A output. Its cause type is: Mechanical failure. Its causes are: Multiple trips due to safety valve issues and the complexity of resuming operations after outages.

title: 2023-24 GenCost consultation draft released – just before Xmas 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "2023-24 GenCost consultation draft released – just before Xmas 2023" posted at 08-01-2024 04:14:00 PM is: The CSIRO released its 2023-24 GenCost consultation draft on 21st December 2023, which compares the costs of different energy sources, highlighting the continued competitiveness of renewables over nuclear energy. The release, coupled with AEMO's 2024 forecasting assumptions update, sparked significant media coverage and social media discussions regarding the economic viability of various energy sources. Submissions on the draft are due by early February 2024, with the final report expected in the second quarter of 2024. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Ongoing analysis and reports influencing future market discussions. Its root cause is: Release of a consultation draft report on energy costs by CSIRO. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 21-12-2023 (release date of the draft). Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Policymakers, energy sector stakeholders, consumer advocates. Its cause type is: Information dissemination. Its causes are: Release of CSIRO's 2023-24 GenCost report, media and social media analysis and reactions, inclusion of pre-2030 integration costs in analysis.

title: A first look at ‘Market Demand’ in South Australia on Saturday 6th January 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A first look at ‘Market Demand’ in South Australia on Saturday 6th January 2024" posted at 07-01-2024 04:10:00 PM is: The article discusses the unforeseen fluctuation in market demand in South Australia on 6th January 2024, highlighting a significant drop in demand between 12:30 and 16:30, followed by a rapid recovery by 19:00. This anomaly was due to the forecasted cloud cover, anticipated to suppress rooftop PV and increase grid demand, not occurring as predicted. The sudden recovery later in the afternoon may have been due to the cloud cover arriving later than initially forecasted. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short term, limited to the specific day of the event. Its root cause is: Misjudgment of cloud cover affecting solar PV forecast. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 07-01-2024. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Forecast discrepancy. Its causes are: Inaccurate prediction of cloud cover affecting rooftop solar PV output.

title: An initial review of Cohuna Solar Farm through 2023 – including bushfire effects (related to electrical equipment onsite?)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "An initial review of Cohuna Solar Farm through 2023 – including bushfire effects (related to electrical equipment onsite?)" posted at 04-01-2024 03:12:00 PM is: The Cohuna Solar Farm, managed by Enel Green Power in Victoria, experienced operational disruptions following a directive from Energy Safe Victoria (ESV), the independent safety regulator, to cease power generation due to a fire risk from specific equipment. The shutdown was particularly tied to a grassfire incident on 21st November 2023, affecting 8 to 10 acres of the site. Following safety measures, operations at a reduced capacity resumed on 17th December 2023. Throughout the year 2023, the solar farm faced several outages, influenced by market changes and equipment issues, which did not directly stem from directives from ESV. The fires at the facility highlight ongoing safety concerns, necessitating further investigation to ensure long-term operational stability.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather, turbine/generator failures, grid/system instability, fuel supply issues (coal, gas), coal-fired policies, gas pipeline failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (approximately 10 days). Its root_cause is: Equipment failure related to specific electrical components causing fire risk. Its accident scale is: Site-specific, affecting 8 to 10 acres. Its dates are: November 21, 2023 (incident); December 8, 2023 (directive issued); December 17, 2023 (operations resumed). Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Equipment and safety compliance. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Grassfire possibly triggered by equipment malfunction on November 21, 2023; Subsequent fire risk prompts ESV directive; Previous fire incidents linked to control boxes and connectors at the site.

title: Two (of four) Major Rectification Outages at Yallourn performed.  Have they improved plant performance (Part 1)?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Two (of four) Major Rectification Outages at Yallourn performed. Have they improved plant performance (Part 1)?" posted at 03-01-2024 03:10:00 PM is: The article discusses the ongoing and planned major rectification outages at Yallourn Power Station, overseen by EnergyAustralia, as a response to previous poor performances and frequent forced outages. Notably, YWPS2 experienced a major rectification over 47 days, but issues such as an 'ID fan motor issue' caused setbacks. The article notes that the other units have scheduled outages planned throughout 2024, with YWPS1 starting in March, YWPS3 having minor outages, and YWPS4 preparing for a major outage in August. The impact of these outages on plant performance is still to be fully determined after their completion. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: primarily from March 2024 onwards as major outages are completed and their results evaluated. Its root_cause is: turbine and generator performance issues requiring rectification. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-01-2024, with specific outage schedules from Q4 2023 to Q4 2024. Its affected region is: Latrobe Valley, Australia. Its affected key users are: EnergyAustralia and grid operators dependent on Yallourn's output. Its cause type is: operational performance deficiencies. Its causes are: forced outages, ID fan motor issues, maintenance delays, and feed pump/tube leak issues.

title: Out with a bang or a whimper? And a puzzle or two.
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Out with a bang or a whimper? And a puzzle or two." posted at 01-01-2024 03:19:00 PM is: On the final day of 2023, the National Electricity Market (NEM) saw record low demand and spot prices in Victoria and South Australia, attributed to high rooftop solar PV generation and low underlying consumption due to benign weather conditions. South Australia experienced its first negative half-hourly operational demand, while Victoria hit a record low at 1,564 MW, significantly impacting spot prices. AEMO issued a market notice regarding an "elevated risk of insufficient demand to maintain a secure operating state," prompting speculation about the implications of very low demand levels on grid stability. Despite these challenges, no further significant actions or notices were issued. The event highlighted challenges with market response and potential issues with under-frequency load shedding and voltage control, compounded by difficulties in predicting small non-scheduled generation impacts.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, specifically on 31 December 2023. Its root_cause is: High levels of rooftop PV generation paired with low demand due to favorable weather conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31 December 2023. Its affected region is: South Australia and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Local electricity consumers and grid operators. Its cause type is: Operational and market conditions. Its causes are: Record low demand driven by high rooftop PV generation and low underlying grid demand; market and grid responses to maintain stability.
